Exterior perimeter maintenance (grass cutting, debris removal, standing water drainage)** | Rodents, flies, birds | Removes outdoor harbourage and attractants within 1-metre buffer zone | Weekly | **Additional recommended practices (1 mark for any one):** - **High-level structural cleaning** of overhead pipes, ceiling voids, and light fixtures (quarterly) to remove dust, cobwebs, and insect webs. - **Pest-o-flash / UV inspection** of stored raw materials to detect hidden insect infestations before they spread to production. **Conclusion sentence (for full marks):** *Thus, a comprehensive, multi-layered sanitation program that addresses food, water, and harbourage simultaneously is the most effective and sustainable strategy for preventing pest infestations in any food processing plant.* --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Choice-based]** *"Sanitation is considered the backbone of pest prevention in food plants. Justify this statement with detailed reasoning, covering the following:"* **(a)** Explain the **economic and regulatory significance** of sanitation in preventing pest infestations. (7 marks) **(b)** Describe a **complete sanitation monitoring and verification system** that a food plant should implement, including key metrics and documentation requirements. (7 marks) --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Economic and Regulatory Significance of Sanitation (7 marks):** **1. Economic Significance (3.5 marks):** - **Cost of Prevention vs. Cost of Infestation:** Implementing a robust sanitation program costs approximately **0.1–0.5% of total production cost**. In contrast, a single major pest infestation can cause losses exceeding **5–10% of annual revenue** through product recall, wasted raw materials, production downtime, and decontamination costs. - **Product Recall Avoidance:** Pest contamination is a leading cause of food product recalls globally. Each recall costs a company an average of **$10 million** in direct costs, plus immeasurable brand damage. - **Pesticide Cost Reduction:** Effective sanitation reduces reliance on chemical pesticides by **60–80%**, saving recurring costs and reducing worker safety risks. - **Insurance and Liability:** Many food safety insurance policies require documented sanitation programs. Non-compliance can void coverage, leaving the company liable for all damages. - **Extended Equipment Life:** Regular cleaning prevents corrosion, biofilm buildup, and mechanical wear, extending equipment life by **20–30%**. **2. Regulatory Significance (3.5 marks):** - **FSSAI Requirements (India):** The Food Safety and Standards (Licensing and Registration of Food Businesses) Regulations, 2011, **mandate** sanitary and hygienic conditions in all food processing facilities. Non-compliance can result in **license cancellation, fines up to ₹10 lakhs, and imprisonment**. - **USFDA Standards:** 21 CFR Part 110 (Current Good Manufacturing Practice) explicitly requires that food plants be constructed and maintained in a sanitary condition to prevent pest harborage and contamination. - **Codex Alimentarius:** The international food code (CAC/RCP 1-1969) lists sanitation as a **prerequisite programme (PRP)** for all food safety management systems. - **Global Certifications:** BRC, FSSC 22000, SQF, and IFS certifications all audit sanitation as a **critical clause**. Failure results in certification denial, which can collapse export markets. - **Consumer Protection Laws:** The Consumer Protection Act, 2019, in India, empowers consumers to file complaints for contaminated food, leading to **class-action lawsuits** and punitive damages. **Conclusion (for full marks):** *Therefore, sanitation is not merely a hygiene practice but a strategic investment with direct economic returns and regulatory necessity.* --- **(b) Complete Sanitation Monitoring and Verification System (7 marks):** **1. Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) (1.5 marks):** Every zone (receiving, processing, packaging, storage, dispatch) must have a **documented, validated SOP** specifying: - Cleaning chemicals (name, concentration, contact time) - Cleaning equipment (type, method) - Frequency (daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly) - Responsible personnel - Safety precautions (PPE, chemical handling) **2. ATP Bioluminescence Testing (1.5 marks):** ATP swab tests provide **real-time cleanliness verification** within minutes. - **Food contact surfaces:** RLU < 100 (Pass); RLU > 100 (Fail) - **Non-food contact surfaces:** RLU < 300 (Pass); RLU > 300 (Fail) - Frequency: **Daily** for high-risk zones, **weekly** for low-risk zones. **3. Visual Inspection Checklists (1 mark):** A **scored checklist** (0 = unsatisfactory, 5 = excellent) is used to evaluate each zone on parameters such as floor cleanliness, wall condition, equipment exterior, drainage condition, and waste management. Inspection is conducted **daily by the sanitation supervisor** and **weekly by the QA manager**. **4. Microbiological Testing (1 mark):** Periodic **swab tests for Total Plate Count (TPC), Coliforms, and yeast/mould** are conducted on food contact surfaces. Acceptance criteria: - TPC: < 100 CFU/cm² (food contact) - Coliforms: < 10 CFU/cm² - Yeast/Mould: < 10 CFU/cm² **5. Pest Activity Trend Analysis (1 mark):** Pest trap data (bait stations, insect light traps, rodent stations) is **logged weekly** and analyzed for trends. A sudden increase in trap counts (> 20% week-on-week) triggers a **root cause investigation** and corrective sanitation action. **6. Documentation and Record Keeping (1 mark):** All sanitation activities, test results, and corrective actions must be **documented and retained** for a minimum of **two years** (or as per regulatory requirement). These records are **mandatory for audits** and serve as legal evidence of due diligence. **Conclusion sentence (for full marks):** *A robust monitoring and verification system transforms sanitation from a subjective cleaning activity into an objective, data-driven, and audit-ready quality assurance process.* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks (KTU Board Examiner's Perspective):** > > 1. **Conflating sanitation with general cleaning.** (–1 mark) Sanitation is a *systematic, validated, documented process* — not casual cleaning. Use the word "program," not "activity." > > 2. **Omitting the pest triangle.** (–1 to –2 marks) Any 7-mark or 14-mark answer on this topic that does NOT mention the three survival needs (food, water, shelter) will be penalized. Examiners consider this the *conceptual core* of the question. > > 3. **Treating pesticides as primary.** (–2 marks) Stating that "pesticides are the main pest control method" is a factual error. Always position **sanitation as primary** and chemicals as a last resort. > > 4. **Ignoring monitoring and verification.** (–1 to –2 marks) A complete answer must include how sanitation *effectiveness* is measured (ATP, visual, microbiological). Skipping this is an incomplete answer. > > 5. **No real-world examples or standards cited.** (–1 mark) Referencing FSSAI, USFDA, BRC, or the 3-3-3 rule adds credibility and earns extra marks. Vague answers are penalized. > > 6. **Poor diagram quality or missing diagram.** (–1 to –2 marks) KTU examiners explicitly reward well-labelled diagrams. Always include a flow diagram of the sanitation cycle or a pest triangle illustration in 14-mark answers. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Role of Sanitation in Preventing Pest Infestations** - **Core Definition:** Sanitation is the *systematic prevention* of pest access to **food, water, and shelter** in a food plant. - **Pest Triangle:** Three survival needs — **Food, Water, Harbourage**. Eliminate all three = no infestation. - **3-3-3 Rule:** Clean within **3 feet**, spills within **3 minutes**, waste within **3 hours**. - **PDCA Cycle:** **Plan → Do → Check → Act** — the operational backbone of any sanitation program. - **Sanitation Hierarchy Position:** **Tier 1 control** (highest effectiveness) in the food safety hierarchy — above mechanical and chemical controls. - **Key Metrics to Memorize:** - ATP: RLU < 100 (contact) / < 300 (non-contact) - Clutter Index: CI < 0.1 - Effectiveness: $E_s$ > 80% = highly effective - RH threshold: < 60% to suppress cockroach breeding - Storage clearance: ≥ 45 cm from wall, ≥ 15 cm from floor - **Six Golden Sanitation Practices:** 1. Daily floor mopping with sanitizer 2. Sealed, scheduled waste bin emptying 3. Drainage cleaning and biofilm removal 4. Equipment disassembly and COP cleaning 5. Crack and crevice sealing 6. Exterior perimeter maintenance - **Regulatory Anchors:** FSSAI (India), 21 CFR Part 110 (USFDA), Codex CAC/RCP 1-1969, BRC, FSSC 22000, SQF. - **Key Phrases for 14-Mark Answers:** - *"Sanitation is the FIRST LINE OF DEFENSE in IPM."* - *"Without sanitation, chemical controls are futile."* - *"Pests infest forgotten places, not clean ones."* - *"A clean plant is a defensible plant."* - **Common Pests Targeted by Sanitation:** - **Cockroaches** → floor cleaning, drain cleaning, crack sealing - **Rats/Mice** → waste management, exterior maintenance, structural sealing - **Flies** → waste bin sealing, drainage, entry point management - **Stored product insects** → equipment COP cleaning, raw material inspection - **Birds** → exterior perimeter, netting, roof maintenance - **KTU Exam Mantra:** Always structure 14-mark answers as: **(1) Definition → (2) Principles (Pest Triangle + PDCA) → (3) Specific Practices (with examples) → (4) Monitoring & Verification → (5) Regulatory & Economic Significance → (6) Conclusion.** - **Most Important One-Liner for the Examiner:** *"Sanitation is not a cost — it is an investment in product safety, regulatory compliance, and brand integrity."*

topics.title: Water Quality and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT747 - FOOD PLANT SANITATION & HYGEINE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Water Quality and Control # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Water Quality** in the context of a food processing plant is defined as the **chemical, physical, biological, and radiological characteristics** of water relative to the requirements of one or more biotic species or to any human need or purpose. It is a measure of the condition of water in relation to its suitability for **potable use, food-contact applications, cleaning-in-place (CIP), and as an ingredient** in food products. **Water Control** refers to the systematic management of water quality through: 1. **Source protection** (borewell, municipal, harvested rainwater) 2. **Treatment regimes** (coagulation, filtration, softening, disinfection) 3. **Distribution system integrity** (preventing re-contamination) 4. **Monitoring and validation** (sampling, testing, record-keeping as per FSSAI and BIS standards) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India)** and **BIS IS 10500:2012**, water used in food processing must be of **potable grade**. The reference parameters for drinking water in India are governed by **IS 14543:2016** (packaged drinking water) and **IS 4251:2014** (packaged natural mineral water). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Restaurant Kitchen" Analogy Imagine a professional restaurant kitchen. Just as a chef insists on the **freshest ingredients, cleanest utensils, and sanitized cutting boards** to produce safe, premium food, a food plant demands **microbiologically safe, chemically balanced, and physically clean water** for every operation — from washing raw materials to formulating the final product. **Why is water the "invisible ingredient"?** - A tomato contains ~94% water; a chicken breast contains ~65% water. - Any contaminant in this water becomes a **direct contaminant in your food**. - Think of water as the **bloodstream of the food plant** — if it is contaminated, contamination spreads to every product it touches. > [!TIP] > **Industry Insight:** A single *E. coli* cell in 100 mL of process water is enough to **fail a finished product batch** during microbial testing. The sensitivity is extreme — water quality is **non-negotiable**. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Regulatory Limit (IS 10500:2012) | |---|---|---|---| | pH | pH | dimensionless | **6.5 – 8.5** | | Total Dissolved Solids | TDS | mg/L | **≤ 500** (max 2000) | | Total Hardness | TH | mg/L as CaCO₃ | **≤ 200** (max 600) | | Turbidity | NTU | Nephelometric Turbidity Units | **≤ 1** (max 5) | | Free Residual Chlorine | FRC | mg/L | **≥ 0.2** (max 1.0) | | Total Coliforms | TC | MPN/100 mL | **0** (must be absent) | | E. coli | EC | MPN/100 mL | **0** (must be absent) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Water Quality Assessment ### Pillar 1 — Physical Parameters - **Turbidity (NTU):** Caused by suspended colloidal particles (clay, silt, organic matter). High turbidity *shields* microbes from disinfectants. - **Colour & Odour:** Indicates dissolved organics, iron, manganese, or industrial contamination. - **Temperature:** Affects microbial growth rate; ideal for *Legionella* growth is **25–45 °C**. ### Pillar 2 — Chemical Parameters - **pH:** Master variable controlling disinfection efficacy. Chlorine is most effective at **pH 6.5–7.5**. - **Total Hardness (TH):** Sum of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions. Causes **scaling** in boilers and heat exchangers. - **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS):** Affects taste and product formulation. Calculated by evaporating a filtered sample at **103–105 °C**. - **Chloride & Sulphate:** Corrosion indicators. - **Heavy metals** (Pb, As, Cd, Hg): Cumulative toxins, must be **<0.01 mg/L** for lead. ### Pillar 3 — Microbiological Parameters - **Total Plate Count (TPC):** < 100 CFU/mL for process water. - **Coliforms & Faecal Streptococci:** Indicator organisms. - **Pathogens of concern:** *Salmonella, E. coli O157:H7, Listeria monocytogenes, Cryptosporidium parvum*. ### Pillar 4 — Radiological Parameters - Alpha and beta emitters must be screened periodically (less common in routine plant testing). ## 2.2 The C-T-S Paradigm (Coagulation–Treatment–Sanitation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "C-T-S"?** It is the engineering shorthand for the water treatment train: > **C**oagulation (chemistry) → **T**reatment (physics: filtration) → **S**anitation (biology: chlorination/UV) ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > All formulas use **mg/L** unless otherwise stated. Volume **V** is in **litres (L)** or **m³** (1 m³ = 1000 L). | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $D = \dfrac{M \times V}{10^6}$ | Dose of chemical (M = mg/L, V in L) → mass in g if V in mL | g | | 2 | $\text{TH} = 2.5 \times [\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.1 \times [\text{Mg}^{2+}]$ | Total Hardness from Ca²⁺ & Mg²⁺ concentrations | mg/L as CaCO₃ | | 3 | $\text{Cl}_2 \text{ dose} = \text{Cl}_2 \text{ demand} + \text{Residual}$ | Chlorine dosing relationship | mg/L | | 4 | $\text{Langlier Index (LI)} = \text{pH}_{\text{actual}} - \text{pH}_{\text{s}}$ | Saturation index; LI > 0 → scaling, LI < 0 → corrosion | dimensionless | | 5 | $\text{Log Reduction} = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)$ | Microbial inactivation (N₀ = initial, N = final count) | log units | | 6 | $\text{CT value} = C \times t$ | Disinfectant concentration × contact time | mg·min/L | | 7 | $\text{BOD}_5 = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f}{P}$ | 5-day BOD of wastewater | mg/L | | 8 | $\text{CTC (mL)} = \dfrac{\text{Chlorine dose (mg/L)} \times \text{Tank volume (L)}}{1000 \times \text{Strength of chlorine solution (\%)} \times 10}$ | Commercial chlorine quantity for dosing | mL | | 9 | $\text{Sludge Volume} = \dfrac{\text{Coagulant dose} \times \text{Flow} \times t}{\text{Solids concentration}}$ | Sludge generation in coagulation | m³/d | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Beverage Industry:** TDS < 50 mg/L is mandatory to avoid flavour carry-over. - **Dairy Plants:** Hard water causes **milk-stone** deposits (Ca/Mg + milk proteins) on heat exchanger plates. - **Meat Processing:** High chlorine residual needed to control *Listeria* biofilms on contact surfaces. - **Brewing:** Calcium is desired for yeast flocculation; iron and chlorine must be **removed** to prevent off-flavours. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Hardness Calculation from Ion Concentrations ### Problem Setup A water sample contains **Ca²⁺ = 80 mg/L** and **Mg²⁺ = 24 mg/L**. Compute the total hardness expressed as CaCO₃ equivalent. ### Concept Background Hardness is conventionally expressed in **mg/L as CaCO₃** because CaCO₃ has a molecular weight of **100 g/mol** — a convenient round number for engineering calculations. The conversion uses the **equivalent weight** concept. ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Recall the equivalent weight of CaCO₃. $$E_{\text{CaCO}_3} = \dfrac{\text{Molar mass}}{2} = \dfrac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ g/eq}$$ **Step 2:** Convert Ca²⁺ concentration to CaCO₃ equivalent. The equivalent weight of Ca²⁺ is: $$E_{\text{Ca}} = \dfrac{40}{2} = 20 \text{ g/eq}$$ Therefore: $$\text{Ca hardness as CaCO}_3 = \text{Ca}^{2+} \text{ (mg/L)} \times \dfrac{E_{\text{CaCO}_3}}{E_{\text{Ca}}} = 80 \times \dfrac{50}{20}$$ $$\text{Ca hardness} = 80 \times 2.5 = 200 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$ **Step 3:** Convert Mg²⁺ concentration to CaCO₃ equivalent. The equivalent weight of Mg²⁺ is: $$E_{\text{Mg}} = \dfrac{24.3}{2} = 12.15 \text{ g/eq}$$ Therefore: $$\text{Mg hardness as CaCO}_3 = 24 \times \dfrac{50}{12.15}$$ $$\text{Mg hardness} = 24 \times 4.12 = 98.8 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$ **Step 4:** Sum the contributions. $$\text{Total Hardness (TH)} = 200 + 98.8 = 298.8 \text{ mg/L as CaCO}_3$$ **Step 5:** Classify the hardness. $$\text{Since TH} = 298.8 > 200 \text{ mg/L}, \text{ water is classified as \textbf{HARD}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** The textbook formula $TH = 2.5 \cdot [Ca^{2+}] + 4.1 \cdot [Mg^{2+}]$ is a **rounded shortcut** (using 4.1 instead of 4.12) — examiners usually accept the rounded value giving **298.4 mg/L as CaCO₃**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Breakpoint Chlorination Dose Calculation ### Problem Setup A food plant water tank has a volume of **50,000 L**. Laboratory jar tests indicate: - Chlorine demand = **1.8 mg/L** - Desired free residual chlorine (FRC) = **0.5 mg/L** - Available chlorine solution strength = **1% (10,000 mg/L)** Calculate: - (a) Total chlorine dose required (mg/L) - (b) Volume of 1% chlorine solution to be added (mL) ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step (a): Total Chlorine Dose** Apply the chlorine demand relationship: $$D_{\text{total}} = \text{Demand} + \text{Residual}$$ $$D_{\text{total}} = 1.8 + 0.5 = 2.3 \text{ mg/L}$$ **Step (b): Volume of 1% Chlorine Solution** The relationship between chlorine dose and commercial chlorine solution volume: $$V_{\text{solution}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{total}} \times V_{\text{tank}}}{C_{\text{solution}}}$$ where $C_{\text{solution}} = 10{,}000 \text{ mg/L}$ for a 1% solution. Substituting values: $$V_{\text{solution}} = \dfrac{2.3 \times 50{,}000}{10{,}000}$$ $$V_{\text{solution}} = \dfrac{115{,}000}{10{,}000} = 11.5 \text{ mL}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing **% strength with mg/L directly**. A **1% chlorine solution = 10,000 mg/L**, not 1 mg/L. Always convert % to mg/L by multiplying by **10,000**. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Log Reduction (Disinfection Efficiency) ### Problem Setup UV disinfection of process water reduces coliform count from **N₀ = 50,000 CFU/100 mL** to **N = 50 CFU/100 mL**. Calculate the log reduction value (LRV). ### Step-by-Step Solution Apply the standard log reduction formula: $$\text{LRV} = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)$$ $$\text{LRV} = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{50{,}000}{50}\right) = \log_{10}(1000)$$ $$\text{LRV} = 3.0 \text{ log units} \quad (\text{equivalent to } 99.9\% \text{ inactivation})$$ > [!NOTE] > **Rule of Thumb:** A **1-log reduction = 90% kill**, **2-log = 99% kill**, **3-log = 99.9% kill**, **4-log = 99.99% kill**. Food plants typically demand **≥ 5-log reduction** for pathogens like *E. coli* O157:H7 in process water. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Disinfection Methods | Method | Dose/Intensity | Contact Time | Log Kill | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Chlorination | 1–5 mg/L | 30 min | 3–5 | Residual, cheap | Trihalomethane (THM) formation | | Ozonation | 1–3 mg/L | 5–10 min | 4–6 | No residual chlorine | High cost, no residual | | UV Irradiation | 30–40 mJ/cm² | < 1 s | 3–4 | No chemical, no taste | No residual, lamp fouling | | Membrane (RO) | 100–300 psi | Continuous | 5–7 | Removes dissolved solids | Expensive, fouling | | Boiling | 100 °C | 15–20 min | 5+ | Simple | Energy-intensive, no residual | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Complete Water Treatment Flow Diagram for a Food Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Water Source
Borewell or Municipal] --> B[Pre-Chlorination
0.5 mg/L Dose] B --> C[Coagulation Tank
Alum 20-60 mg/L + Lime] C --> D[Flocculation Basin
Gentle Stirring 30 min] D --> E[Sedimentation Tank
4 hour Retention] E --> F[Sand Filter
Multimedia Filtration] F --> G[Activated Carbon Filter
Removes Odour and Chlorine] G --> H[Water Softener
Ion Exchange Resin] H --> I[Breakpoint Chlorination
Dose 2-3 mg/L] I --> J[Storage Tank
FRC Maintained 0.2-0.5 mg/L] J --> K{Quality Test} K -->|Pass| L[Distribution Network
SS316 Piping] K -->|Fail| M[Re-treatment Loop] M --> C L --> N[Process Water] L --> O[Cleaning In Place CIP] L --> P[Boiler Feed] L --> Q[Potable Use] ``` ## 4.2 Breakpoint Chlorination Curve (Chlorine Residual Behaviour) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Initial Dose Added] --> B[Region A: Free Chlorine
Destroys Bacteria] B --> C[Region B: Chloramines Form
Combined Chlorine] C --> D[Region C: Chloramines Oxidised
Residual Dips to Minimum] D --> E[Region D: Breakpoint Achieved
Free Cl2 Reappears] E --> F[Region E: Excess Free Cl2
Sustained Residual] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The **breakpoint** is the chlorine dose at which combined chlorine (chloramines) is completely destroyed. Operating *to the left* of the breakpoint leaves **combined residual** (slow, weak disinfectant). Operating *to the right* of the breakpoint delivers **free residual chlorine** (the potent, fast-acting form). ## 4.3 Microbiological Indicator Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Microbiological Quality] --> B[Total Plate Count TPC] A --> C[Total Coliforms TC] A --> D[Faecal Coliforms FC] A --> E[Specific Pathogens] C --> C1[E coli Detection] C --> C2[Faecal Streptococci] E --> E1[Salmonella spp] E --> E2[Listeria monocytogenes] E --> E3[Staph aureus] ``` ## 4.4 HACCP-Based Water Quality Control Block Diagram ```mermaid block-beta columns 3 block:ID1["Hazard Identification in Water"] block:ID2["CCP 1: Source Protection"] block:ID3["CCP 2: Treatment Process"] block:ID4["CCP 3: Distribution Integrity"] block:ID5["Critical Limits FRC 0.2 to 0.5 mg per L"] block:ID6["Monitoring pH TDS Microbial Tests"] block:ID7["Corrective Action Rechlorination or Flushing"] block:ID8["Verification Daily Testing and Audits"] block:ID9["Record Keeping FSSAI Compliance"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define the term "Total Hardness of Water" and state its significance in food plant operations.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Total Hardness (TH) is the total concentration of divalent metallic cations, predominantly **calcium (Ca²⁺)** and **magnesium (Mg²⁺)** ions, expressed as **mg/L of CaCO₃ equivalent** in water. **Significance in food plants (1.5 marks):** 1. Causes **scaling** in boilers, heat exchangers, and pasteurizers → reduced heat transfer efficiency. 2. Forms **milk-stone** deposits in dairy plants (Ca/Mg + milk proteins). 3. Affects **product texture and flavour** in brewing and beverages. 4. Limits the **cleaning efficiency** of detergents (Ca²⁺ reacts with soap to form scum). > [!NOTE] > **Marking Scheme:** 1.5 marks for definition + 1.5 marks for significance. Examiners want at least **three valid industry impacts** for full marks. ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is "Breakpoint Chlorination"? Why is it essential in food plant water treatment?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Breakpoint chlorination is the point at which the **chlorine dose is sufficient to oxidize all combined chlorine (chloramines) and ammonia**, after which any additional chlorine appears as **free residual chlorine (FRC)**. It represents the **minimum dose** needed to obtain a persistent free chlorine residual. **Essentiality (1.5 marks):** 1. Free residual chlorine provides **immediate and sustained disinfection** against *E. coli*, *Salmonella*, and coliforms. 2. FSSAI and BIS mandate a **minimum FRC of 0.2 mg/L** in food contact water. 3. Provides a **measurable safety buffer** in the distribution network against re-contamination. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, every Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Students must attempt **one full choice** (either Q A or Q B). --- ### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **"With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the complete water treatment process for a food processing plant. Discuss the role of each stage in ensuring water potability."** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Water Treatment Train with Diagram **Model Solution:** A modern food plant employs a **multi-barrier treatment approach**. The stages are: | Stage | Process | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Source Selection & Pre-screening** | Removes large debris; isolates contaminated sources. | | 2 | **Pre-chlorination** (0.5 mg/L) | Controls biological growth in raw water mains. | | 3 | **Coagulation–Flocculation** (Alum + Lime) | Destabilizes and aggregates colloidal turbidity. | | 4 | **Sedimentation** (4-h retention) | Allows flocs to settle under gravity. | | 5 | **Filtration** (Sand + Activated Carbon) | Removes remaining suspended particles, odour, colour. | | 6 | **Softening** (Ion Exchange) | Removes Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ to prevent scaling. | | 7 | **Breakpoint Chlorination** (2–3 mg/L) | Achieves free residual chlorine of 0.5 mg/L. | | 8 | **Storage & Distribution** (SS316 piping) | Maintains potability up to point of use. | **Why multi-barrier?** - Each barrier addresses a **different class of contaminant** — physical, chemical, and microbiological. - A **single barrier failure** does not result in unsafe water (defence-in-depth principle). **Diagram (3 marks):** Students must draw a **labelled block diagram** showing the sequential flow from raw water → process water. The diagram in **Section 4.1** above may be reproduced in the answer script with proper labelling. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Potability Standards and Monitoring **Model Solution:** **Potability Parameters (as per IS 10500:2012 / FSSAI):** - pH: **6.5 – 8.5** - TDS: ≤ **500 mg/L** (desirable) - Turbidity: ≤ **1 NTU** (desirable) - Free Residual Chlorine: **0.2 – 1.0 mg/L** - Total Coliforms: **0 per 100 mL** (must be absent) - E. coli: **0 per 100 mL** (must be absent) - Heavy metals (Pb, As, Cd): **< 0.01 mg/L** **Monitoring Schedule:** - **Hourly:** Free residual chlorine, pH, temperature. - **Daily:** Turbidity, TDS, microbiological swab tests of tap points. - **Weekly:** Total plate count, total coliforms, hardness. - **Monthly:** Full chemical + radiological audit. - **Annually:** Third-party FSSAI compliance audit. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often **omit the monitoring frequency**. Mere listing of parameters scores only 3/7 marks. Examiners expect a **structured monitoring schedule with frequencies** for full credit. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **"Explain the principle of disinfection by chlorination. With a neat sketch of the breakpoint chlorination curve, describe the different regions and discuss its practical importance in food plant water systems."** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Chlorination Chemistry and Disinfection Principle **Model Solution:** **Principle (3 marks):** Chlorination disinfects water through the action of **hypochlorous acid (HOCl)** and **hypochlorite ion (OCl⁻)**, collectively known as **free available chlorine**. When Cl₂ is added to water: $$Cl_2 + H_2O \longrightarrow HOCl + HCl$$ The HOCl dissociates based on pH: $$HOCl \rightleftharpoons H^+ + OCl^-$$ HOCl is **80–100 times more effective** as a disinfectant than OCl⁻. It inactivates microbes by: 1. **Oxidising** the sulphydryl (–SH) groups of microbial enzymes. 2. **Damaging** the cell membrane and nucleic acids. 3. Causing **cytoplasmic leakage** and cell lysis. **Practical Considerations (4 marks):** - **pH Control:** Maintain pH 6.5–7.5 to keep HOCl dominant. - **Contact Time:** Minimum 30 minutes for ≥ 3-log kill. - **Turbidity Limit:** < 1 NTU; high turbidity *shields* microbes. - **Organic Load:** High ammonia → formation of chloramines (combined chlorine). #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Breakpoint Chlorination Curve **Model Solution:** The **breakpoint chlorination curve** plots **residual chlorine (Y-axis)** against **chlorine dose applied (X-axis)**. It has **five distinct regions**: | Region | Chlorine Form | Mechanism | Practical Implication | |---|---|---|---| | **A** | Free Cl₂ (HOCl/OCl⁻) | Destroys bacteria and oxidizes Fe²⁺/Mn²⁺ | Initial disinfection | | **B** | Combined Cl₂ (Chloramines) | Reacts with NH₃ → NH₂Cl, NHCl₂ | Residual declines | | **C** | Dips to minimum | Chloramines oxidised to N₂ gas | "Trough" of curve | | **D** | Breakpoint reached | All combined Cl₂ destroyed | Free Cl₂ reappears | | **E** | Free Cl₂ rises linearly | Excess Cl₂ beyond demand | **Operating zone** | **Practical Importance:** 1. Ensures water has **free residual chlorine**, not weak combined chlorine. 2. The **breakpoint dose** must be determined by **jar testing** for each water source. 3. Operating **before the breakpoint** → ineffective disinfection. 4. Operating **beyond the breakpoint** → waste of chlorine, taste issues, THM formation. **Sketch (2 marks):** A properly labelled X-Y graph showing dose vs. residual with all five regions marked. The schematic in **Section 4.2** may be redrawn as a 2D graph in the answer. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2:** Most students **forget to draw the curve** or label the five regions. Examiners specifically allocate **2 marks for the sketch** and **2 marks for region identification**. Skipping these leads to losing 4 marks. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print & Pin):** - [ ] **Definition:** Water quality = chemical + physical + biological + radiological suitability. - [ ] **FSSAI mandate:** All process water must be of **potable grade** (IS 10500:2012). - [ ] **Hardness formula:** $\text{TH} = 2.5\,[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.1\,[\text{Mg}^{2+}]$ (in mg/L as CaCO₃). - [ ] **Hardness Classification:** Soft < 75, Moderately Hard 75–150, Hard 150–300, Very Hard > 300. - [ ] **Optimum pH for chlorination:** 6.5 – 7.5 (HOCl dominant). - [ ] **FRC limit:** Minimum **0.2 mg/L** at the **furthest point** of distribution. - [ ] **Breakpoint = Demand + Residual**; it is the **minimum dose** for sustained disinfection. - [ ] **Log Reduction:** 1-log = 90%, 3-log = 99.9%, 5-log = 99.999%. - [ ] **CT value** = Concentration (mg/L) × Time (min) — used to validate disinfection. - [ ] **Disinfection Hierarchy:** Chlorination (cheap, residual) > Ozonation (fast, no residual) > UV (no chemical) > RO (complete barrier). - [ ] **Monitoring Frequencies:** Hourly (FRC, pH); Daily (Turbidity, TDS); Weekly (Microbial); Monthly (Chemical); Annually (Audit). - [ ] **HACCP CCPs for water:** Source protection → Treatment → Distribution → Verification. - [ ] **Indicator organism:** E. coli (must be **0 per 100 mL** in process water). - [ ] **Scaling vs. Corrosion:** Use **Langelier Saturation Index (LI)** — positive = scaling, negative = corrosion. - [ ] **Material of construction for piping:** **SS 316** is the food-grade standard for water distribution.

topics.title: Importance of Water Quality in Food Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT747 - FOOD PLANT SANITATION & HYGEINE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Importance of Water Quality in Food Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Quality in Food Processing** is defined as the chemical, physical, microbiological, and sensory suitability of water used as a direct ingredient, a processing medium, a cleaning agent, or a utility in food manufacturing operations, conforming to the prescribed national and international potable-water standards (BIS IS 10500:2012, WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality, 4th Ed., 2017, and Codex Alimentarius CX-S 227-2001).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEFTT747 – Module 2)**, water quality transcends mere "potability" — it is a **Critical Control Point (CCP)** under the **Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Point (HACCP)** system and a prerequisite programme (PRP) mandated by **ISO 22000:2018** and **FSSC 22000 v6** for all food business operators (FBOs).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water is the Silent Ingredient" Concept

Imagine you are baking a cake. You measure flour, sugar, eggs, and butter very carefully. But suppose the water you add (or wash your hands with, or that condenses on your cold surfaces) is contaminated with coliform bacteria or carries an off-flavor of chlorine. **The cake is ruined — not because of what you added, but because of what you ignored.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Unseen Chef**
> Water in a food plant behaves like an *unseen chef* working silently in the background. It mixes, washes, boils, chills, and transports. If the unseen chef is "sick" (contaminated) or "unhygienic" (hard, turbid, chemically imbalanced), every dish the head chef prepares is compromised. Therefore, monitoring water quality is equivalent to supervising this unseen chef constantly.

### 1.3 Why Water Quality is Non-Negotiable in Food Processing

Water is the **most extensively used raw material** in the food industry. Its uses can be classified into four functional categories:

| Functional Use | Typical Examples | Required Quality Grade |
|---|---|---|
| **Ingredient Water** | Soups, beverages, dough, brines, syrups | Potable (drinking water standard) |
| **Process Water** | Blanching, cooking, cooling, pasteurization | Potable, often softened/deionized |
| **Cleaning Water (CIP – Clean-In-Place)** | Sanitization of equipment, pipelines, surfaces | Potable + disinfected |
| **Utility Water** | Boiler feed, cooling towers, fire-fighting | Treated (softened, deaerated) — *not for product contact* |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Per Module 2 of PEFTT747, students must be able to enumerate the sources of water, identify contaminants, describe treatment methods, and justify the legal/safety rationale for water-quality control in food plants.

### 1.4 The Three Pillars of Water Quality

```
                    ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                    │   WATER QUALITY TRIAD        │
                    │   (Three Pillars)            │
                    └──────────────────────────────┘
                               │
        ┌──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┐
        │                      │                      │
   ┌────▼─────┐         ┌──────▼──────┐        ┌──────▼──────┐
   │ PHYSICAL │         │ CHEMICAL    │        │ MICRO-      │
   │          │         │             │        │ BIOLOGICAL  │
   │ •Turbidity│        │ •pH         │        │ •Total Coli │
   │ •Colour  │         │ •Hardness   │        │ •Fecal Coli │
   │ •Odour   │         │ •Chloride   │        │ •E. coli    │
   │ •TDS     │         │ •Residual Cl│        │ •TVC       │
   │ •Temp.   │         │ •Heavy metals│       │ •Pathogens │
   └──────────┘         └─────────────┘        └─────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Fact:** Approximately **70% of the Earth's surface is water**, but less than **1% is fresh, accessible, and suitable for food processing** without extensive treatment. This scarcity amplifies the importance of *reuse, recycling, and in-plant water management*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" — Strategic Importance of Water Quality

The importance of water quality in food processing can be analyzed across **five strategic dimensions**:

1. **Public Health Protection** — Prevention of waterborne disease outbreaks (cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A, dysentery, giardiasis).
2. **Product Safety & Shelf-Life** — Microbial load in water directly determines the safety and storage stability of finished products.
3. **Product Quality & Sensory Integrity** — Hardness, chlorine, iron, and TDS affect taste, color, texture, and aroma.
4. **Regulatory Compliance** — FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India, 2006 Act), USFDA 21 CFR Part 117, EU Regulation 852/2004, and Codex Alimentarius all mandate potable water for food contact.
5. **Equipment Integrity & Process Efficiency** — Hard water causes scale in boilers, heat exchangers, and pasteurizers; corrosive water damages stainless steel (SS 304/316) surfaces, creating harborage sites for *Listeria* and *Pseudomonas* biofilms.

### 2.2 The "How" — Water-Quality Parameter Framework

#### 2.2.1 Physical Parameters

- **Turbidity** — Measure of suspended colloidal matter. Measured in **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units)**. *Acceptable limit: ≤ 1 NTU* (WHO), *≤ 5 NTU* (BIS acceptable, ≤ 1 NTU for filtration effluents).
- **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** — Sum of all dissolved substances. *Acceptable limit: ≤ 500 mg/L* (BIS), *≤ 1000 mg/L* (WHO upper bound).
- **Color & Odour** — Qualitative. *Acceptable: Colour ≤ 5 Hazen units; Odour unobjectionable.*
- **Temperature** — Influences microbial growth kinetics and solubility of gases.

#### 2.2.2 Chemical Parameters

- **pH** — Measure of hydrogen-ion activity. For food processing, the **optimal pH is 6.5 – 8.5** (BIS IS 10500:2012). Deviations affect enzymatic activity, microbial growth, and corrosion rates.
- **Total Hardness** — Concentration of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions, expressed as **mg/L CaCO₃**. *Acceptable: ≤ 200 mg/L; Permissible: ≤ 600 mg/L.*
- **Residual Chlorine** — Free chlorine remaining after disinfection. *Acceptable: ≥ 0.2 mg/L at point of delivery* (FSSAI/WHO).
- **Heavy Metals** — Lead (Pb ≤ 0.01 mg/L), Arsenic (As ≤ 0.01 mg/L), Cadmium (Cd ≤ 0.003 mg/L), Mercury (Hg ≤ 0.001 mg/L), Chromium (Cr⁶⁺ ≤ 0.05 mg/L).
- **Fluoride, Nitrate, Sulphate** — Each has statutory limits; nitrate (NO₃⁻ ≤ 45 mg/L) is especially critical due to methemoglobinemia risk in infants.

#### 2.2.3 Microbiological Parameters

| Parameter | Unit | Acceptable Limit (FSSAI/BIS) |
|---|---|---|
| Total Coliforms | MPN/100 mL | **0** (Nil) |
| Faecal Coliforms / *E. coli* | MPN/100 mL | **0** (Nil) |
| Total Viable Count (TVC) | CFU/mL | ≤ 50 (drinking), varies for process water |
| *Faecal Streptococci* | MPN/100 mL | **0** |
| *Clostridium* spores | MPN/100 mL | 0 – 10 |
| *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* | per 100 mL | 0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Food-Plant Water:** Water that comes in contact with food or food-contact surfaces **must** be of at least **potable quality** at the point of use — *not* merely at the municipal inlet.

### 2.3 Sources of Water and Their Quality Profile

| Source | Typical Quality | Treatment Required |
|---|---|---|
| **Municipal / Public Supply** | Treated, chlorinated; generally meets BIS | Minimal — residual Cl₂ check, pH, hardness |
| **Deep Borehole (Groundwater)** | Low turbidity, high hardness, possible Fe/Mn, no coliforms | Softening, iron removal, disinfection |
| **Surface Water (River/Lake)** | High turbidity, organic matter, coliforms, possible pesticides | Coagulation → Filtration → Disinfection |
| **Rainwater Harvest** | Soft, low TDS, microbiologically unsafe | Disinfection (UV/Cl₂), filtration |
| **Reclaimed / Recycled (in-plant)** | Variable; high organics, biofilm risk | Coagulation + Membrane (UF/RO) + UV/Cl₂ |

### 2.4 Water-Use Intensity in Major Food Sectors

| Food Industry Sector | Water Use (L per kg product, approx.) |
|---|---|
| Soft drinks / Beverages | 2 – 5 |
| Dairy (milk processing) | 3 – 10 |
| Fruit & Vegetable Canning | 30 – 60 |
| Breweries | 4 – 8 |
| Slaughterhouses | 300 – 800 |
| Poultry processing | 20 – 40 |
| Bakeries | 1 – 3 |

> **Source:** FAO Water Reports / Industry benchmarks. These figures underscore why **water efficiency and reuse strategies** (CIP optimization, counter-current rinsing, membrane bioreactors) are now central to food-plant sustainability audits.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas and numerical relationships are **high-yield for KTU ESE numerical and short-answer questions**.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Hardness | $TH = 2.5 \times [Ca^{2+}] + 4.1 \times [Mg^{2+}]$ | mg/L as CaCO₃ | Convert Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ concentrations |
| 2 | Langelier Saturation Index (LSI) | $LSI = pH_{actual} - pH_{saturation}$ | dimensionless | Predict CaCO₃ scaling tendency (LSI > 0 = scaling) |
| 3 | pH calculation (strong acid) | $pH = -\log_{10}[H^{+}]$ | — | Acidity assessment |
| 4 | Free Residual Chlorine (break-point) | Demand-adjusted dose | mg/L Cl₂ | Disinfection efficacy |
| 5 | CT Concept (Disinfection) | $CT = C_{residual} \times t_{contact}$ | mg·min/L | Chlorine inactivation of microbes |
| 6 | Chlorine Demand | $Cl_{demand} = Cl_{dose} - Cl_{residual}$ | mg/L | Sizing of chlorinator |
| 7 | Log Reduction | $Log\ Reduction = \log_{10}(N_0/N_t)$ | log units | Microbial inactivation efficiency |
| 8 | Dilution factor | $C_f = C_i \times (V_i / V_f)$ | mg/L | Mixing / dilution calculations |
| 9 | % Removal efficiency | $\eta = \left( \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \right) \times 100$ | % | Treatment performance |
| 10 | Flow conversion | $Q = A \times v$ | m³/s | Pipe flow / dosing pump sizing |

**Critical Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
- $pH_{saturation}$ depends on Ca²⁺, alkalinity, TDS, and temperature.
- For effective chlorination, **pH must be < 8.0** (HOCl dominates below 7.5; OCl⁻ above 7.5 — HOCl is 80× more germicidal).
- Free residual Cl₂ for food-plant disinfection: **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L** after ≥ 30 min contact.
- *E. coli* inactivation: 99.9% (3-log) at CT = 1.5 mg·min/L at 25 °C, pH 7.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Langelier Saturation Index (LSI) is used by food-plant engineers to:
- Decide whether boiler-feed water needs softening (LSI > 0 → scaling risk).
- Determine if pipework will corrode (LSI < -2 → aggressive water).
- Optimize CIP detergent concentrations and rinse cycles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic primarily addresses **CO2: "Apply principles of sanitation to design and operate food plant water systems"** and **CO3: "Evaluate water treatment methods for food safety compliance."** Mastery of the formula sheet and parameter limits is essential.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Total Hardness Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):**
A water sample from a food plant has the following analysis:
- Calcium (Ca²⁺) = 80 mg/L
- Magnesium (Mg²⁺) = 24 mg/L
- Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) = 122 mg/L

Calculate the **total hardness (TH)** and **carbonate hardness (CH)**, and classify the water.

**Step 1 — Convert Ca²⁺ to CaCO₃ equivalent.**
The equivalent weight of CaCO₃ is 50 g/mol, and Ca²⁺ is 20 g/mol.

$$
[Ca^{2+}]_{as\ CaCO_3} = \frac{Equivalent\ weight\ of\ CaCO_3}{Equivalent\ weight\ of\ Ca^{2+}} \times [Ca^{2+}]
$$

$$
[Ca^{2+}]_{as\ CaCO_3} = \frac{50}{20} \times 80 = 2.5 \times 80 = 200\ mg/L\ as\ CaCO_3
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Mg²⁺ to CaCO₃ equivalent.**
Equivalent weight of Mg²⁺ = 12.15 g/mol.

$$
[Mg^{2+}]_{as\ CaCO_3} = \frac{50}{12.15} \times 24 = 4.115 \times 24 = 98.77\ mg/L\ as\ CaCO_3
$$

> **Logic:** Since CaCO₃ is the universal reference, every cation is expressed as the *mass of CaCO₃ that contains the same number of moles of the cation*.

**Step 3 — Total Hardness.**

$$
TH = [Ca^{2+}]_{as\ CaCO_3} + [Mg^{2+}]_{as\ CaCO_3} = 200 + 98.77 = 298.77\ mg/L\ as\ CaCO_3
$$

**Step 4 — Carbonate Hardness (CH).**
Bicarbonate alkalinity is expressed as CaCO₃.

$$
Alkalinity_{as\ CaCO_3} = \frac{50}{61} \times [HCO_3^-] = 0.82 \times 122 = 100.04\ mg/L\ as\ CaCO_3
$$

Since TH > Alkalinity, carbonate hardness = Alkalinity = **100.04 mg/L as CaCO₃**.

**Step 5 — Non-Carbonate Hardness (NCH).**

$$
NCH = TH - CH = 298.77 - 100.04 = 198.73\ mg/L\ as\ CaCO_3
$$

**Step 6 — Classification.**
According to BIS IS 10500:2012:
- 0 – 75 mg/L → Soft
- 75 – 150 mg/L → Moderately hard
- 150 – 300 mg/L → Hard
- > 300 mg/L → Very hard

Since TH = 298.77 mg/L, the water is classified as **Hard water (approaching the Very Hard threshold)**.

**Conclusion for Food Plant Use:**
Such water is unsuitable as boiler feed (scaling risk) and requires **softening** (ion exchange / lime-soda) before utility use. It may be acceptable for cleaning but **softening is mandatory** for process water to prevent product-quality issues (e.g., mineral deposition in canned vegetables).

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Chlorination Demand & CT Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):**
A food plant treats 50,000 L/day of well water with chlorine. The chlorine dose applied is 2.5 mg/L, and the residual after 30 minutes is 0.6 mg/L.
Calculate:
(a) Chlorine demand
(b) Contact-time (CT) value
(c) Log reduction of *E. coli* achieved, given that 3-log reduction of *E. coli* requires CT = 0.3 mg·min/L at pH 7, 25 °C.

**Step 1 — Chlorine Demand.**

$$
Cl_{demand} = Cl_{dose} - Cl_{residual} = 2.5 - 0.6 = 1.9\ mg/L
$$

**Step 2 — Contact Time (CT).**

$$
CT = C_{residual} \times t = 0.6\ mg/L \times 30\ min = 18\ mg \cdot min/L
$$

**Step 3 — Log Reduction of *E. coli*.**

Given 3-log reduction requires CT = 0.3 mg·min/L, the log reduction achieved is:

$$
Log\ Reduction = \frac{CT_{actual}}{CT_{required\ for\ 3-log}} \times 3 = \frac{18}{0.3} \times 3 = 60 \times 3 = 180
$$

> **Note:** Theoretically, this is the *linear extrapolation*. In practice, disinfection kinetics follow **Chick-Watson law**, and CT values saturate. For this KTU problem, the linear interpretation is accepted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** CT requirements increase at lower temperatures and higher pH. For every 10 °C drop, CT roughly doubles (Arrhenius-type behaviour). This is why cold-water disinfection in winter requires either higher doses or longer contact times.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Dilution / Mixing Calculation

**Problem:**
A food plant must dilute a 200 mg/L chlorine stock solution to 5 mg/L working concentration for surface sanitization. If the final volume needed is 100 L, how much stock solution is required?

**Step 1 — Apply conservation of mass.**

$$
C_i \times V_i = C_f \times V_f
$$

$$
200 \times V_i = 5 \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for V_i.**

$$
V_i = \frac{5 \times 100}{200} = 2.5\ L
$$

**Step 3 — Verification.**

$$
C_f = \frac{200 \times 2.5}{100} = \frac{500}{100} = 5\ mg/L \ \checkmark
$$

> **Answer:** Add **2.5 L of stock** to **97.5 L of potable water** to obtain 100 L of 5 mg/L working sanitizing solution.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Water-Quality Parameter Decision Grid

| Quality Concern | Indicator | Food-Plant Implication | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hard water (TH > 200) | Scale on heat exchangers | Reduced thermal efficiency, biofilm under scale | Ion-exchange softening |
| High residual Cl₂ (> 0.5 mg/L) | Chlorine off-flavour in beverages | Product rejection, consumer complaints | Dechlorination (ascorbic acid, activated carbon) |
| Coliform presence | MPN > 0 | CCP failure, product recall risk | Hyperchlorination + UV |
| High TDS (> 500 mg/L) | Salty/metallic taste | Sensory defect | RO treatment |
| Low pH (< 6.5) | Pipe corrosion | Metal leaching, equipment damage | pH correction (NaOH dosing) |
| High turbidity (> 5 NTU) | Particulates | Disinfection inefficiency, deposit on products | Coagulation-flocculation-sand filtration |
| Iron > 0.3 mg/L | Red-brown staining, metallic taste | Product discoloration | Aeration + sand filtration / MnO₂ |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Water Quality Pillars and Their Food-Plant Impact

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Water Source] --> B[Inlet Quality Assessment]
    B --> P1[Physical Tests]
    B --> P2[Chemical Tests]
    B --> P3[Microbiological Tests]

    P1 --> P1a[Turbidity]
    P1 --> P1b[Colour and Odour]
    P1 --> P1c[Total Dissolved Solids]
    P1 --> P1d[Temperature]

    P2 --> P2a[pH]
    P2 --> P2b[Hardness]
    P2 --> P2c[Residual Chlorine]
    P2 --> P2d[Heavy Metals]

    P3 --> P3a[Total Coliforms]
    P3 --> P3b[Faecal Coliforms]
    P3 --> P3c[Total Viable Count]
    P3 --> P3d[Pathogen Screen]

    P1a --> Q{Quality OK?}
    P1b --> Q
    P1c --> Q
    P1d --> Q
    P2a --> Q
    P2b --> Q
    P2c --> Q
    P2d --> Q
    P3a --> Q
    P3b --> Q
    P3c --> Q
    P3d --> Q

    Q -- Yes --> R[Release to Process]
    Q -- No --> S[Reject to Treatment]
    S --> T[Treat and Recycle]
    T --> B

    R --> U1[As Ingredient]
    R --> U2[As Process Water]
    R --> U3[As Cleaning Water CIP]
    R --> U4[As Utility Feed]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Typical Food-Plant Water Treatment Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RAW[Raw Water Source] --> S1[Intake Screening]
    S1 --> S2[Coagulation Flocculation Tank]
    S2 --> S3[Sand Filtration]
    S3 --> S4[Activated Carbon Filter]
    S4 --> S5[Water Softener Ion Exchange]
    S5 --> S6[Cartridge Micron Filter 5 micron]
    S6 --> S7[Chlorine Dosing 0.2 to 0.5 mg per L]
    S7 --> S8[Storage Tank]
    S8 --> S9[UV Disinfection Unit]
    S9 --> S10[Distribution to Plant]

    S10 --> O1[Ingredient Water]
    S10 --> O2[Process Water]
    S10 --> O3[CIP Cleaning]
    S10 --> O4[Boiler Feed]
    S10 --> O5[Cooling Tower]

    O1 -.-> M1[Daily pH Cl2 Micro Tests]
    O2 -.-> M1
    O3 -.-> M1
    O4 -.-> M2[Weekly Hardness Test]
    O5 -.-> M3[Micro and Legionella Monthly]

    M1 --> R[Records and HACCP Documentation]
    M2 --> R
    M3 --> R
```

### 4.3 Concept Map — Importance of Water Quality (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WQ[Water Quality in Food Processing] --> I1[Public Health Safety]
    WQ --> I2[Product Quality]

    WQ --> I5[Sustainability and Reuse]

    I1 --> D1[Prevents Waterborne Illnesses]

    I1 --> D3[Removes Chemical Contaminants]

    I2 --> Q1[Optimizes Taste and Odour]
    I2 --> Q2[Prevents Discoloration]
    I2 --> Q3[Maintains Texture]

    I3 --> R1[FSSAI Compliance]
    I3 --> R2[Codex Alimentarius Standards]
    I3 --> R3[ISO 22000 PRPs]

    I4 --> E1[Reduces Scale Formation]
    I4 --> E2[Prevents Corrosion]
    I4 --> E3[Extends Boiler Life]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    D1 --> CL1[Critical Limit Residual Cl2 0.2 to 0.5 mg per L]
    D1 --> CL2[Microbial Limit Zero Coliforms per 100 mL]

    D2 --> P1[pH 6.5 to 8.5]
    D2 --> P2[Hardness less than 200 mg per L]

    D3 --> PT2[No visible particulates]
