- ✅ **QA-Sanitation Pyramid:** GMP (foundation) → PRP + SSOP (daily ops) → HACCP (hazard control) → ISO/FSSC (system certification).
- ✅ **Seven HACCP Principles** — hazard analysis, CCPs, critical limits, monitoring, corrective actions, verification, documentation.
- ✅ **SSOPs** must include objective, frequency, chemicals (ppm + pH + temperature + contact time), tools, procedure, responsibility, verification, corrective action, records.
- ✅ **LRV formula:** $LRV = \log_{10}(N_0) - \log_{10}(N_f)$ ; FSSAI benchmark for *Salmonella*/*Campylobacter* = **≥ 5-log**.
- ✅ **D-value and Z-value** are temperature/time based microbial kill parameters.
- ✅ **Control Chart Limits:** $UCL = \bar{X} + 3\sigma$ ; $LCL = \bar{X} - 3\sigma$ — use sample SD with $(n-1)$.
- ✅ **ATP swab threshold** in well-controlled sanitation: ≤ **30 RLU** (process), ≤ **50 RLU** (acceptable upper limit).
- ✅ **PDCA (Deming Cycle)** = Plan → Do → Check → Act — the engine of continual QA improvement.
- ✅ **EMP** = air + surfaces + water + personnel + drains — all five must be routinely sampled.
- ✅ **CAPA** (Corrective & Preventive Action) is mandatory whenever a sanitation deviation occurs.
- ✅ **Key certifications in food sanitation QA:** ISO 22000, FSSC 22000 v6, BRC v9, SQF Edition 9.
- ✅ **Indian regulatory anchors:** FSSAI Schedule 4 (GMP), IS 2491 (sanitation in food industries), Codex Alimentarius.
- ✅ **QA documentation must be retained** for the product's shelf-life + one year (minimum) for traceability.
- ✅ **Course Outcome Mapping:** This topic primarily satisfies **CO₄** (Apply QA principles to maintain food plant sanitation) at **Bloom's Level 4 – Analyze** and partially **CO₅** (Evaluate sanitation compliance) at **Level 5 – Evaluate**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Auditing and Inspection Procedures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT747 - FOOD PLANT SANITATION & HYGEINE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Auditing and Inspection Procedures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU's **PEFTT747 – Food Plant Sanitation & Hygiene** (2024 Scheme), **Auditing and Inspection Procedures** are defined as the systematic, documented, and objective processes used to evaluate, verify, and ensure that a food manufacturing facility, its personnel, processes, equipment, and documentation comply with established food safety regulations, internal policies, and internationally recognized standards such as **Codex Alimentarius, ISO 22000, FSSC 22000, BRC Global Standard for Food Safety, SQF, and FSMA (Food Safety Modernization Act)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Auditing** = A *systematic, independent, and documented* review of a food safety management system (FSMS) to determine whether it conforms to specified criteria and is effectively implemented.
>
> **Inspection** = A *physical, on-site examination* of the facility, equipment, personnel practices, and raw/finished products to verify compliance with statutory and regulatory requirements.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **college semester examination system**:

- **Inspection** is like the **surprise visit by the HOD** walking into the lab to check whether students are wearing aprons, gloves, and following protocols. It is *observational, immediate, and corrective*.
- **Auditing** is like the **NAAC or NBA accreditation team** that arrives once in a few years, reviews documents (attendance registers, lab manuals, project reports), interviews staff, and gives a formal verdict — *Accredited / Not Accredited / Conditionally Accredited*.

Similarly, in a food plant:
- **Inspections** are routine (daily/weekly) walk-throughs by supervisors or regulatory officers (FSSAI, FDA, USDA).
- **Audits** are scheduled, formal reviews by trained internal auditors or third-party certification bodies.

### 1.3 Key Metrics and Standards

The following globally recognized standards govern food plant auditing and inspection:

| Standard / Code | Issuing Body | Primary Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **ISO 22000:2018** | ISO (International Organization for Standardization) | FSMS Requirements |
| **FSSC 22000 v6** | Foundation for Food Safety Certification | GFSI-recognized FSMS |
| **BRCGS Issue 9** | Brand Reputation Compliance Global Standards | Product safety & quality |
| **SQF Edition 9** | Safe Quality Food Institute | Hazard analysis & HACCP |
| **Codex Alimentarius** | FAO/WHO | International food standards |
| **FSMA (FDA, USA)** | U.S. FDA | Preventive controls & traceability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under the **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) Act, 2006**, every food business operator (FBO) is mandated to obtain **FSSAI License/Registration** and is subject to **periodic inspections and audits** by Designated Officers (DOs). Non-compliance may attract penalties under **Section 50–59 of the FSS Act**.

### 1.4 Classification of Audits and Inspections

**Audits** are classified as:

1. **Internal Audits (First-Party Audits)** – Conducted by or on behalf of the organization itself.
2. **External Audits**:
   - **Second-Party Audits** – Conducted by customers or buyers on their suppliers.
   - **Third-Party Audits** – Conducted by independent certification bodies (e.g., TÜV SÜD, SGS, Bureau Veritas).
3. **Regulatory Audits** – Conducted by government authorities (FSSAI, FDA, EFSA).

**Inspections** are classified as:

1. **Routine Inspection** – Pre-planned, periodic walk-throughs.
2. **Surprise/Random Inspection** – Unannounced checks by regulators.
3. **Follow-up Inspection** – Conducted after corrective actions to verify closure of non-conformities.
4. **Complaint-based Inspection** – Triggered by consumer or trade complaints.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Audit Process — Structured Theoretical Breakdown

A complete food safety audit follows a **logical, sequential flow**. Each phase is critical and feeds into the next.

#### Phase 1: Audit Planning and Preparation
- **Objective Setting:** Define the scope (entire plant vs. a specific line), criteria (FSSC 22000, BRC), and boundaries (which departments, which time period).
- **Audit Plan Preparation:** Develop a **Gantt-chart-style schedule** listing activities, auditor assignments, and time allocations.
- **Document Review (Pre-Audit Study):** Review the Food Safety Manual, HACCP plan, SOPs, SSOPs, PRPs (Prerequisite Programs), previous audit reports, CAPAs (Corrective and Preventive Actions), and pest control records *before* arriving on-site.
- **Checklist Preparation:** Develop structured checklists aligned with the relevant standard's clauses.
- **Team Formation:** Appoint a **Lead Auditor** (auditor-in-charge) and **Audit Team Members** with domain expertise (microbiology, sanitation, GMP).

#### Phase 2: Opening Meeting
- Introduce the audit team, confirm the scope, agree on the schedule, and clarify confidentiality protocols.
- Senior management (Plant Head, QA Manager) typically attends.

#### Phase 3: On-Site Audit Execution
This is the core phase, comprising three sub-activities:

**a) Document Review (On-site):**
Examine current versions of SOPs, training records, calibration certificates, cleaning logs, temperature records, traceability records, and supplier approvals.

**b) Physical Inspection / Plant Walk-through:**
The auditor physically walks through the facility following the **product flow** (from raw material receiving to finished goods dispatch), observing:
- Building and equipment condition
- Personnel hygiene and GMP compliance
- Cleaning and sanitation practices (SSOP verification)
- Pest control measures
- Storage and warehousing conditions
- Cross-contamination risks

**c) Interviews:**
Structured questioning of operators, supervisors, and managers to verify *implementation* (not just documentation). The auditor asks "Show me how you…" questions.

#### Phase 4: Audit Findings Classification
Findings are categorized into:

- **Critical Non-Conformity (CNC / Major):** A lapse that poses an immediate food safety risk or shows systemic failure. **May lead to suspension of certification.**
- **Major Non-Conformity:** A significant gap requiring corrective action within a defined timeframe (typically 30–60 days).
- **Minor Non-Conformity:** A low-risk observation that does not immediately compromise food safety but needs correction.
- **Observation / Opportunity for Improvement (OFI):** A suggestion for best-practice improvement; not a non-conformity.

#### Phase 5: Closing Meeting
The Lead Auditor presents findings, confirms understanding, and agrees on timelines for corrective action submission.

#### Phase 6: Audit Report and Follow-up
- A formal **Audit Report** is issued within an agreed number of working days (typically 10–15).
- The auditee submits **Corrective Action Plans (CAPAs)**.
- The auditor verifies effectiveness (often via document review; sometimes via a follow-up visit).

### 2.2 Risk Priority Number (RPN) — Quantitative Audit Severity Assessment

Auditors often quantify risk using the **FMEA-derived Risk Priority Number**:

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

where:
- **S** = Severity (1–10): How severe is the consequence of the failure?
- **O** = Occurrence (1–10): How frequently does the failure occur?
- **D** = Detection (1–10): How likely is the failure to be detected before reaching the consumer? (Higher score = harder to detect)

Higher RPN indicates higher food safety risk requiring **immediate corrective action**.

### 2.3 Inspection Procedure — Theoretical Framework

An inspection follows a **shorter, observation-heavy** workflow:

1. **Authorization & Legal Notice:** The inspector carries an ID and authorization letter.
2. **Entry and Declaration:** The inspector announces the purpose and scope.
3. **Document Examination:** FSSAI license, water analysis reports, pest control contracts, lab test reports.
4. **Physical Examination:** Equipment, premises, product samples.
5. **Sampling and Testing:** Food, water, or swab samples may be collected under **Section 38/47 of the FSS Act**.
6. **Observation Recording:** Written notes, photographs, video (where permitted).
7. **Show-Cause / Improvement Notice:** For non-compliances, a written notice is issued.
8. **Reporting:** A formal inspection report is filed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **FSSAI's Food Safety and Compliance System (FoSCoS)**, all inspections must be logged digitally, and **Annual Returns** must be filed by every licensed FBO.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Metrics Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Boundary | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Dimensionless; each factor 1–10 | Used in HACCP revalidation & audit prioritization |
| **Critical Control Point (CCP) Decision Tree** | Q1–Q4 sequential YES/NO logic (Codex) | Logical / Boolean | Determines which steps need CCP monitoring |
| **Audit Compliance %** | $\displaystyle \text{Compliance \%} = \frac{\text{Compliant Items}}{\text{Total Items Audited}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | Higher is better; benchmark ≥ **95 %** |
| **CAPA Closure Rate** | $\displaystyle \text{Closure Rate} = \frac{\text{CAPAs Closed On Time}}{\text{Total CAPAs Raised}} \times 100$ | Percent (%) | Industry benchmark ≥ **90 %** |
| **Audit Finding Density** | $\displaystyle \text{Findings/hour} = \frac{\text{Total Findings}}{\text{Audit Duration (hours)}}$ | Findings per hour | Indicates audit thoroughness |
| **Microbiological Limit (APC)** | $\text{Aerobic Plate Count}$ | CFU/g or CFU/ml | Per **FSSAI / ICMSF** standards |
| **Coliform Limit** | $\text{Most Probable Number (MPN)}$ | MPN/g | e.g., ≤ **10 MPN/g** for pasteurized milk |
| **pH Boundary (Acid Foods)** | $\text{pH} \leq 4.6$ | pH units | Defines "low-acid" vs. "acidified" foods |
| **Water Activity (a<sub>w</sub>) Boundary** | $\text{a}_{w} \leq 0.85$ | Dimensionless | Below this, *Clostridium botulinum* cannot grow |
| **Lethality (F-value)** | $\text{F}_{T}^{z} = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{(T(t)-T_{\text{ref}})/z}\, dt$ | Minutes | Used in retort sterilization calculations |
| **Sanitizer Concentration (Chlorine)** | Typical range **50–200 ppm** | ppm (mg/L) | For food-contact surface sanitation |
| **Wash Water Temperature (Dishware)** | ≥ **82 °C** (WHO / NSF) | °C | Commercial dishwashing standard |
| **HACCP Re-validation Frequency** | At least **annually** or after any significant change | Time-based | Required by Codex / FSSC 22000 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the **CCP Decision Tree** (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4) and the **RPN formula** — these are highly tested topics in the 14-mark ESE questions.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FSSC 22000 audits** are mandatory for exporting food products to the EU, USA, and Japan — without certification, the product is rejected at the port.
- **BRCGS Issue 9** compliance is required by major retailers like Tesco, Walmart, and Carrefour.
- **FSSAI audits** are legally enforceable in India under the **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides exhaustive analytical step-by-step derivations for key audit-related calculations and decision frameworks that frequently appear in KTU university examinations.

### 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Audit Compliance Percentage

The **Audit Compliance Percentage** quantifies the degree to which a food facility conforms to a chosen standard's requirements. The derivation proceeds as follows.

**Step 1: Define the Total Number of Auditable Items (T)**

Let the audit checklist contain $n$ clauses (e.g., BRCGS Issue 9 has 318 clauses). Each clause is treated as a single auditable item.

$$T = n$$

**Step 2: Define the Compliant Items (C)**

For each clause, the auditor evaluates whether the facility is fully compliant ($y_i = 1$) or non-compliant ($y_i = 0$). Then:

$$C = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$$

**Step 3: Define the Compliance Percentage (C%)**

By definition, the compliance percentage is the ratio of compliant items to total items, expressed as a percentage:

$$\text{C\%} = \frac{C}{T} \times 100$$

**Step 4: Substitute the Summation Form**

$$\text{C\%} = \left( \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i}{n} \right) \times 100$$

**Step 5: Worked Numerical Example**

Suppose an FSSC 22000 audit covers **10 critical clauses**, and the facility is compliant on **8** of them. Then:

$$C\% = \left( \frac{8}{10} \right) \times 100 = 80\%$$

Since FSSC 22000 requires **100 %** compliance on critical clauses for certification, this facility **fails** the audit and must implement corrective actions.

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Risk Priority Number (RPN) for Audit Findings

The **RPN** is the most commonly tested quantitative audit tool in KTU exams.

**Step 1: Assign Severity (S)**

Severity reflects the magnitude of the food safety consequence. The auditor assigns a score on a scale of 1 (negligible) to 10 (catastrophic, e.g., *Salmonella* outbreak).

**Step 2: Assign Occurrence (O)**

Occurrence reflects how often the failure is observed. Scale: 1 (very rare) to 10 (very frequent).

**Step 3: Assign Detection (D)**

Detection reflects the probability of the failure being caught before reaching the consumer. Note that a **higher D-score means LOWER detectability** (counter-intuitive but standard in FMEA). Scale: 1 (almost certain detection) to 10 (almost impossible to detect).

**Step 4: Multiply**

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

**Step 5: Worked Example — Handwashing Failure in a Bakery**

| Parameter | Justification | Score |
|---|---|---|
| **S (Severity)** | Pathogen transfer could cause illness | **8** |
| **O (Occurrence)** | Workers occasionally skip handwashing | **5** |
| **D (Detection)** | Supervisor does not routinely observe | **7** |

Then:

$$\text{RPN} = 8 \times 5 \times 7 = 280$$

**Step 6: Risk Decision**

RPN > **200** → **High Priority** — Corrective action must be implemented immediately (e.g., install handwashing stations with timers and supervisory monitoring).

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation — CAPA Closure Effectiveness Rate

**Step 1: Identify the Total CAPAs Raised in a Period**

$$\text{Total CAPAs} = N_{\text{total}}$$

**Step 2: Identify the CAPAs Closed On-Time**

A CAPA is considered closed on-time if (a) the corrective action is implemented, (b) verified by the auditor, and (c) within the agreed deadline.

$$\text{Closed on time} = N_{\text{closed}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Closure Rate**

$$\text{Closure Rate (\%)} = \left( \frac{N_{\text{closed}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \right) \times 100$$

**Step 4: Worked Example**

Suppose a facility raised **50 CAPAs** in the last year, and **42** were closed within their deadlines. Then:

$$\text{Closure Rate} = \left( \frac{42}{50} \right) \times 100 = 84\%$$

Industry benchmark is ≥ **90 %**. Therefore, this facility is **below benchmark** and must improve its corrective action management system.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CCP Decision Tree (Codex Alimentarius)

The **CCP Decision Tree** is used during hazard analysis to determine whether a process step is a Critical Control Point. It consists of **four sequential questions (Q1–Q4)**:

**Q1:** Do preventive control measures exist for the identified hazard?

**Q2:** Is this step specifically designed to eliminate or reduce the likely occurrence of the hazard to an acceptable level? (If YES → **CCP**. If NO → go to Q3.)

**Q3:** Could contamination with the identified hazard(s) occur in excess of acceptable level(s), or could it increase to unacceptable level(s)?

**Q4:** Will a subsequent step eliminate or reduce the identified hazard to an acceptable level? (If NO → **CCP**. If YES → **Not a CCP**.)

**Logical Flow Representation:**

$$\text{Q1 (NO)} \rightarrow \text{Modify process to introduce control, then re-evaluate}$$

$$\text{Q1 (YES)} \rightarrow \text{Q2}$$

$$\text{Q2 (YES)} \rightarrow \text{CCP}$$

$$\text{Q2 (NO)} \rightarrow \text{Q3}$$

$$\text{Q3 (NO)} \rightarrow \text{Not a CCP}$$

$$\text{Q3 (YES)} \rightarrow \text{Q4}$$

$$\text{Q4 (NO)} \rightarrow \text{CCP}$$

$$\text{Q4 (YES)} \rightarrow \text{Not a CCP}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sanitizer Concentration Verification (Chlorine-based)

For food-contact surfaces, free chlorine concentration must be verified. The required concentration depends on the application:

**Step 1:** Identify the application context.

| Application | Target Free Chlorine (ppm) |
|---|---|
| Hand sanitizing (food handlers) | **50–100 ppm** |
| General surface sanitization | **100–200 ppm** |
| Drain and floor sanitization | **200–400 ppm** |
| CIP (Clean-in-Place) of equipment | **150–200 ppm** |

**Step 2:** Sample test using **DPD (N,N-Diethyl-p-phenylenediamine) colorimetric method**.

**Step 3:** Compare measured value to target. If below target → re-dilute or re-prepare sanitizer solution.

> [!WARNING]
> **Chlorine concentrations above 200 ppm** on food-contact surfaces can cause **off-flavors, corrosion of stainless steel, and residue toxicity**. Always rinse with potable water after sanitizing.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Food Safety Audit Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Audit Trigger Scheduled or Surprise"] --> B1["Phase 1 Audit Planning"]
    B1 --> B2["Define Scope Criteria Boundaries"]
    B2 --> B3["Form Audit Team Lead Auditor and Members"]
    B3 --> B4["Prepare Checklist and Document Review"]
    B4 --> C1["Phase 2 Opening Meeting"]
    C1 --> D1["Phase 3 On-Site Audit Execution"]
    D1 --> D2["Document Review SOPs Records"]
    D1 --> D3["Plant Walk-through Product Flow"]
    D1 --> D4["Personnel Interviews Show Me How"]
    D2 --> E1["Phase 4 Audit Findings Classification"]
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    E1 --> E2["Critical Non-Conformity Immediate Risk"]
    E1 --> E3["Major Non-Conformity 30 to 60 Days"]
    E1 --> E4["Minor Non-Conformity"]
    E1 --> E5["Observation OFI"]
    E2 --> F1["Phase 5 Closing Meeting"]
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1
    E5 --> F1
    F1 --> G1["Phase 6 Audit Report Issued"]
    G1 --> G2["CAPA Submission by Auditee"]
    G2 --> G3["CAPA Verification by Auditor"]
    G3 --> H1{"CAPA Effective"}
    H1 -- Yes --> H2["Audit Closed Successfully"]
    H1 -- No --> H3["Re-Audit or Escalation"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Inspection Procedure (FSSAI Framework)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Inspector Arrives with Authorization"] --> nodeB["Display ID and Legal Notice"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Examine FSSAI License and Documents"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Review Water Pest and Lab Reports"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Physical Plant Inspection"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Sample Collection FSS Act Sec 38"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Interview Staff and Workers"]
    nodeG --> nodeH{"Compliance Status"}
    nodeH -- Satisfactory --> nodeI["Issue Compliance Certificate"]
    nodeH -- Unsatisfactory --> nodeJ["Issue Improvement Notice"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Schedule Follow-up Inspection"]
    nodeK --> nodeL{"Compliance Restored"}
    nodeL -- Yes --> nodeI
    nodeL -- No --> nodeM["Legal Action Prosecution under FSS Act"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — HACCP CCP Decision Tree (Codex Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    qA["Q1 Do Preventive Controls Exist"] -- NO --> rA["Modify Step and Re-evaluate"]
    qA -- YES --> qB["Q2 Step Eliminates Hazard to Safe Level"]
    qB -- YES --> ccpA["CCP Identified"]
    qB -- NO --> qC["Q3 Could Contamination Exceed Acceptable Level"]
    qC -- NO --> ncpA["Not a CCP"]
    qC -- YES --> qD["Q4 Will a Later Step Eliminate the Hazard"]
    qD -- NO --> ccpB["CCP Identified"]
    qD -- YES --> ncpB["Not a CCP"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Audit Finding Severity Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fA["Audit Finding Logged"] --> fB{"Immediate Food Safety Risk"}
    fB -- YES --> fC["CRITICAL Major Suspension Possible"]
    fB -- NO --> fD{"Systemic Failure or Repeated Issue"}
    fD -- YES --> fE["MAJOR 30 to 60 Day Closure"]
    fD -- NO --> fF{"Low Risk Documentation Gap"}
    fF -- YES --> fG["MINOR Correct Within 90 Days"]
    fF -- NO --> fH["OBSERVATION OFI Best Practice"]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — CAPA Cycle (Corrective and Preventive Action)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cA["Non-Conformity Identified"] --> cB["Root Cause Analysis 5 Why Ishikawa"]
    cB --> cC["Define Corrective Action"]
    cC --> cD["Implement Corrective Action"]
    cD --> cE["Verify Effectiveness"]
    cE --> cF{"Effective"}
    cF -- Yes --> cG["CAPA Closed and Documented"]
    cF -- No --> cB
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **First-Party, Second-Party, and Third-Party audits** in a food safety management system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **First-Party Audit (Internal Audit):** Conducted by the organization's own trained auditors to evaluate its own FSMS against internal standards or external criteria like ISO 22000. Purpose: self-assessment and continuous improvement.
- **Second-Party Audit:** Conducted by a *customer* on its *supplier* to verify that the supplier meets contractual food safety and quality requirements before procurement. Purpose: supplier qualification.
- **Third-Party Audit:** Conducted by an *independent, accredited certification body* (e.g., TÜV SÜD, SGS) to issue a formal certification (FSSC 22000, BRCGS, SQF). Purpose: independent certification and market access.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q2.** What is a **Critical Non-Conformity** in the context of a food safety audit? Give one example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Critical Non-Conformity** is an audit finding that represents an *immediate food safety risk* or demonstrates *systemic failure* of the FSMS. It typically results in **suspension or withdrawal of certification** until verified closure.

**Example:** Discovery of *Listeria monocytogenes* in a ready-to-eat food product at the dispatch stage, indicating breakdown of sanitation and HACCP controls.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice A]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **complete procedure for conducting a food safety audit** in a food processing plant, with reference to ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **classification of audit findings** and explain the **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** method with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q.A(a): Food Safety Audit Procedure (7 marks)**

The audit procedure consists of **six sequential phases**:

**1. Audit Planning (1 mark):**
- Define scope, criteria, and boundaries.
- Form the audit team (Lead Auditor + Technical Experts).
- Develop the audit plan and checklist aligned with ISO 22000 clauses (Context, Leadership, Planning, Support, Operation, Performance Evaluation, Improvement).
- Review pre-audit documents: HACCP plan, PRPs, SOPs, previous audit reports, CAPAs.

**2. Opening Meeting (1 mark):**
- Confirm scope, schedule, and confidentiality.
- Introduce the audit team to senior management.
- Agree on communication protocols and safety rules.

**3. On-Site Audit Execution (2 marks):**
This comprises three parallel activities:
- **Document Review:** Verify current SOPs, training records, calibration logs, cleaning records, traceability documents, supplier approvals.
- **Physical Plant Walk-through:** Follow the product flow from raw material receipt → processing → packaging → dispatch. Observe GMP, SSOP, sanitation, pest control, equipment condition, personnel hygiene.
- **Interviews:** Question operators and managers to verify *implementation* (not just paper compliance). Use open-ended "show me" questions.

**4. Audit Findings Classification (1 mark):**
Findings are classified into Critical, Major, Minor, or Observation.

**5. Closing Meeting (1 mark):**
Lead Auditor presents findings, confirms factual accuracy, and agrees on CAPA submission timelines.

**6. Audit Report and Follow-up (1 mark):**
A formal written report is issued within 10–15 working days. The auditee submits CAPAs, which the auditor verifies for effectiveness. Follow-up audits may be scheduled.

---

### **Model Solution for Q.A(b): Audit Findings Classification & RPN (7 marks)**

**Classification of Audit Findings (3.5 marks):**

| Category | Description | Action Timeline |
|---|---|---|
| **Critical** | Immediate food safety risk or systemic failure | Suspension / 0–7 days |
| **Major** | Significant gap, not immediate risk | 30–60 days |
| **Minor** | Low-risk documentation gap | 60–90 days |
| **Observation / OFI** | Best-practice suggestion | No formal closure required |

**Risk Priority Number (RPN) Method (3.5 marks):**

$$\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$$

- **S (Severity):** 1–10 scale based on health consequence.
- **O (Occurrence):** 1–10 scale based on observed frequency.
- **D (Detection):** 1–10 scale (higher = harder to detect).

**Worked Example:** Pest droppings found in raw material storage area.

| Parameter | Score |
|---|---|
| Severity (pathogen entry risk) | **8** |
| Occurrence (pest activity present) | **6** |
| Detection (no routine inspection) | **7** |

$$\text{RPN} = 8 \times 6 \times 7 = 336$$

**RPN = 336 > 200 → HIGH PRIORITY** — Immediate pest control treatment, sealing of entry points, and intensified monitoring is required.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice B]**

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe the **regulatory inspection procedure under FSSAI**, listing the powers of the Food Safety Officer (FSO) and the legal provisions under the FSS Act, 2006. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **CAPA cycle** with reference to food plant sanitation, and compute the CAPA closure rate for a facility that raised 60 CAPAs in a year and closed 51 on time. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Q.B(a): FSSAI Inspection Procedure (7 marks)**

**1. Legal Authority (2 marks):**
The **Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006** empowers the **Food Safety Officer (FSO)** and ** Designated Officer (DO)** to inspect any food business operator (FBO) under **Section 38** of the Act. The FSO must carry a valid ID card and authorization letter.

**2. Powers of the FSO (3 marks):**
Under **Sections 38, 39, 40, 47, and 50** of the FSS Act, the FSO can:
- **Enter and inspect** any premises where food is manufactured, stored, or sold.
- **Examine records**, books, and documents relating to food business.
- **Collect samples** of food, water, or swabs for analysis (Section 47).
- **Seize** articles suspected to be unsafe (Section 39).
- **Issue Improvement Notices** for non-compliance (Section 40).
- **Detain** foodstuffs suspected to be adulterated or misbranded.
- **Initiate prosecution** before the Adjudicating Officer or Tribunal (Section 50).

**3. Inspection Procedure (2 marks):**
- Announcement of purpose and verification of FSSAI license.
- Document review (FSSAI registration, water analysis, pest control records, lab reports).
- Physical plant walk-through (GMP, sanitation, hygiene, storage).
- Sample collection and sealing as per legal protocol.
- Recording observations and issuing written notices.

---

### **Model Solution for Q.B(b): CAPA Cycle & Closure Rate Calculation (7 marks)**

**CAPA Cycle (3.5 marks):**

$$\text{Non-Conformity} \rightarrow \text{Root Cause Analysis} \rightarrow \text{Corrective Action Plan} \rightarrow \text{Implementation} \rightarrow \text{Verification} \rightarrow \text{Closure or Re-iterate}$$

- **Identification:** Non-conformity is logged during audit or inspection.
- **Root Cause Analysis:** Techniques used include the **5-Why Analysis** and **Ishikawa Fishbone Diagram**.
- **Corrective Action Definition:** Specific, measurable action with owner and deadline.
- **Implementation:** Action is executed (e.g., sanitation protocol update).
- **Verification:** Auditor or QA Manager confirms effectiveness through re-inspection or testing.
- **Closure:** Documented; if ineffective, the cycle restarts.

**CAPA Closure Rate Calculation (3.5 marks):**

Given:
- Total CAPAs raised, $N_{\text{total}} = 60$
- CAPAs closed on time, $N_{\text{closed}} = 51$

Applying the formula:

$$\text{Closure Rate (\%)} = \left( \frac{N_{\text{closed}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \right) \times 100$$

$$\text{Closure Rate (\%)} = \left( \frac{51}{60} \right) \times 100 = 85\%$$

**Interpretation:** Industry benchmark is ≥ **90 %**. The facility is **below benchmark** by **5 percentage points**, indicating gaps in its corrective action management system.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly lose marks by **confusing Inspection with Audit** — remember: Inspection is *observational/regulatory*; Audit is *systematic/conformity-based*.
> - In RPN calculation questions, students often assign **D inversely**. **Higher D-score = harder to detect** (this is *correct* in FMEA logic).
> - For CAPA Closure Rate, always express the answer in **percentage (%)**, not as a decimal.
> - For CCP Decision Tree, you must write the **flow Q1 → Q2 → Q3 → Q4**, not just the conclusion.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Audit** = systematic, documented, conformity-based review of FSMS.
- **Inspection** = physical, observational, regulatory verification.
- **Six Phases of an Audit:** Planning → Opening Meeting → Execution → Findings → Closing Meeting → Report & Follow-up.
- **Four Types of Audits:** First-Party (Internal), Second-Party (Customer), Third-Party (Certification), Regulatory (Government).
- **Four Types of Inspections:** Routine, Surprise, Follow-up, Complaint-based.
- **Audit Finding Categories:** Critical, Major, Minor, Observation (OFI).
- **RPN Formula:** $S \times O \times D$, where each parameter ranges 1–10; threshold typically ≥ 200 = high priority.
- **CCP Decision Tree:** Q1 → Q2 → Q3 → Q4 logic (Codex Alimentarius).
- **FSSAI Act, 2006:** Sections 38 (Inspection), 39 (Seizure), 40 (Notice), 47 (Sampling), 50 (Prosecution).
- **Sanitizer Standards:** Chlorine 50–200 ppm for food-contact surfaces; rinse with potable water afterward.
- **HACCP Re-validation:** At least annually or after any significant process change.
- **Audit Compliance % Benchmark:** ≥ 95 % for food safety audits.
- **CAPA Closure Rate Benchmark:** ≥ 90 % on-time closure.
- **Key Standards:** ISO 22000, FSSC 22000, BRCGS, SQF, Codex, FSMA, FSSAI.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal and External Audits for Sanitation and Hygiene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT747 - FOOD PLANT SANITATION & HYGEINE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Internal and External Audits for Sanitation and Hygiene

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Audit**, in the context of food plant sanitation and hygiene, is a **systematic, independent, and documented process** for obtaining objective evidence (records, statements of fact, or other information) and evaluating it objectively to determine the extent to which specified criteria (regulatory standards, internal SOPs, ISO norms, customer specifications) are fulfilled.

According to **ISO 19011:2018 (Guidelines for auditing management systems)**, an audit is characterized by three pillars:
1. **Systematic** – follows a structured, planned methodology.
2. **Independent** – the auditor is impartial and free from conflict of interest.
3. **Documented** – findings, evidence, and conclusions are recorded.

**Internal Audit (First-Party Audit):** Conducted by, or on behalf of, the organization itself for management review and internal compliance verification. In KTU 2024 terminology, this is *"an audit performed by the food business operator's own trained personnel against the organization's own Food Safety Management System (FSMS) requirements."*

**External Audit (Second and Third-Party Audit):**
- **Second-Party Audit** – Conducted by customers or by a contracted auditor on behalf of the customer to verify contractual sanitation requirements.
- **Third-Party Audit** – Conducted by an independent certification body (e.g., FSSC 22000, BRCGS, SQF, ISO 22000) to certify the food plant against an internationally recognized standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Sanitation audits are distinct from quality audits. While quality audits focus on product conformity, **sanitation audits specifically evaluate the effectiveness of cleaning, disinfection, pest control, personal hygiene, facility design, and waste management practices** that prevent food contamination.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a student preparing for the KTU Semester Exam:

- **Internal Audit** is like doing a **self-test or mock exam** at home. You check your own understanding, identify weak topics (e.g., Module 4 sanitation), and improve before the real test. You set the questions, you grade yourself, but you must be **honest** to get value.

- **External Audit (Second-Party)** is like your **classmate quizzing you** on the topics they think are important. Their perspective may differ from yours — they focus on what *they* need assurance about.

- **External Audit (Third-Party)** is like the **official University examination** conducted by an external examiner appointed by the university. The examiner uses a standard answer key (ISO standards, FSSC requirements), grades you impartially, and issues a pass/fail certificate. Your diploma depends on it.

In all cases, the **sanitation audit** is the "exam" that verifies whether your food plant is producing safe food under hygienic conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Audit Metric:** Audit findings are quantified using a **Non-Conformity (NC) Index**:
> $$\text{NC Index} = \frac{\text{Number of Non-Conformities Raised}}{\text{Total Audit Hours}}$$
> A benchmark value of **< 0.5 NCs per audit hour** is generally considered acceptable in mature FSMS-certified plants.

### 1.3 Categories of Sanitation Audits in Food Plants

| Audit Type | Auditor | Purpose | Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pre-operational Sanitation Audit** | Internal team | Verifies cleaning effectiveness BEFORE production starts | Daily/Shift change |
| **Operational Sanitation Audit** | Internal team | Monitors hygiene during production | Continuous/Walking audits |
| **Environmental Monitoring Audit** | Internal/External | Reviews pathogen swab results, ATP tests | Weekly/Monthly |
| **Pest Control Audit** | External (Pest Control Operator) | Verifies Integrated Pest Management (IPM) compliance | Monthly |
| **Regulatory Audit** | FSSAI / FDA / External | Legal compliance check | Annual/Unannounced |
| **Certification Audit** | External CB | ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000 / BRCGS certification | Annual (Surveillance) + 3-yearly (Recertification) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Internal Sanitation Audit – Operational Logic

An **Internal Sanitation Audit** is the backbone of any effective FSMS. Per **FSSC 22000 Version 6** and **ISO 22000:2018 Clause 9.2**, the organization must conduct internal audits at planned intervals.

#### Step-by-Step Internal Audit Logic:

1. **Establish the Audit Program (Plan)** – Define scope, frequency, methods, responsibilities.
2. **Select Auditors** – Choose trained personnel **independent of the area being audited** (the QC supervisor cannot audit the QC lab's hygiene).
3. **Prepare Audit Checklist** – Tailor to the specific zone (high-risk vs. low-risk).
4. **Conduct On-Site Verification** – Walk-through, interview, document review, observation, sampling.
5. **Document Findings** – Classify into:
   - **Critical Non-Conformity (CNC)** – Immediate food safety risk; production halt.
   - **Major Non-Conformity (MNC)** – Significant deviation; corrective action within 7 days.
   - **Minor Non-Conformity (mNC)** – Isolated incident; corrective action within 30 days.
   - **Observation / Opportunity for Improvement (OFI)** – Best practice suggestion.
6. **Issue Audit Report** – Within 5 working days of the audit.
7. **Track Corrective and Preventive Actions (CAPA)** – Close the loop.
8. **Management Review Input** – Feed results into the **Management Review Meeting** (Clause 9.3 of ISO 22000).

### 2.2 The External Sanitation Audit – Operational Logic

External audits follow a more formal, standardized protocol. The **"Why"** behind external audits is to provide **independent assurance** to regulators, customers, and consumers that the plant's hygiene controls are credible.

#### Sub-types:

- **Second-Party (Customer) Audit** – Many multinational food brands (Walmart, Nestlé, Unilever) mandate suppliers to pass their proprietary sanitation audits. Failure can lead to **supplier de-listing**.

- **Third-Party (Certification) Audit** – A **Certification Body (CB)** accredited by an accreditation body (e.g., NABCB in India, UKAS in UK, ANAB in USA) audits the plant against a published standard.

**Major GFSI-Recognized Standards (2024):**
1. **FSSC 22000 v6** – Combines ISO 22000 + ISO/TS 22002-1 (Prerequisite Programs)
2. **BRCGS Issue 9** – British Retail Consortium Global Standard for Food Safety
3. **SQF Edition 9** – Safe Quality Food Institute standard
4. **IFS Food Version 8** – International Featured Standards
5. **ISO 22000:2018** – Generic FSMS standard

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Unit / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| **Audit Effectiveness** | $E_a = \dfrac{\text{NC Closed on Time}}{\text{Total NC Raised}} \times 100\%$ | Target: $\geq 95\%$ |
| **Sanitation Compliance Rate** | $C_s = \dfrac{\text{Compliant Checkpoints}}{\text{Total Checkpoints Audited}} \times 100\%$ | Target: $\geq 90\%$ |
| **Non-Conformity Density** | $\rho_{NC} = \dfrac{\text{Total NCs}}{\text{Audit Hours}}$ | Benchmark: $\rho_{NC} \le 0.5 \text{ hr}^{-1}$ |
| **Audit Coverage** | $K_a = \dfrac{\text{Area Audited (m}^2)}{\text{Total Plant Area (m}^2)} \times 100\%$ | Target: $K_a = 100\%$ per cycle |
| **CAPA Closure Time (CCT)** | $CCT = T_{close} - T_{raise}$ | Critical: $\le 24\text{ h}$; Major: $\le 7\text{ d}$; Minor: $\le 30\text{ d}$ |
| **ATP RLU Threshold** | Relative Light Units reading | Pass: $\le 30$ RLU; Fail: $> 30$ RLU (clean equipment) |
| **Microbial Swab Limit (TVC)** | Total Viable Count | Acceptable: $\le 100 \text{ CFU/cm}^2$ (food contact surfaces) |
| **Pathogen Absence** | Salmonella / Listeria / E.coli O157:H7 | Must be **Absent in 25 g or 100 cm²** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Remember that FSSC 22000 v6 mandates **unannounced audits** at least once in a 3-year certification cycle, and surveillance audits are conducted **annually**. This is a high-yield fact for 14-mark questions.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Regulatory Compliance:** Avoids FSSAI penalties (up to ₹10 lakh under FSS Act 2006) and product recalls.
- **Market Access:** GFSI-recognized certification is mandatory to supply to major retailers globally.
- **Brand Protection:** A single sanitation failure can damage decades of brand equity (e.g., 2008 China melamine scandal).
- **Insurance Premiums:** Plants with effective sanitation audits get reduced product liability insurance premiums.
- **Operational Efficiency:** Audits identify hygiene bottlenecks, reducing downtime and rework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Process Mapping

### 3.1 Internal Audit Cycle — The PDCA Framework

Internal sanitation audits are governed by the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle**, which mirrors **Clause 9.2 of ISO 22000:2018**.

$$
\text{PDCA Cycle} = \begin{cases} \textbf{P (Plan):} & \text{Define audit program, schedule, scope, criteria} \\ \textbf{D (Do):} & \text{Conduct audits using checklists, interviews, observations} \\ \textbf{C (Check):} & \text{Analyze findings, classify NCs, issue report} \\ \textbf{A (Act):} & \text{Implement CAPA, verify effectiveness, feed into Mgmt Review} \end{cases}
$$

#### Step-by-Step Execution of an Internal Sanitation Audit:

**Step 1 — Pre-Audit Preparation**

The Lead Auditor prepares:
- Approved Audit Plan (date, time, scope, areas).
- Audit Checklist customized for the zone (e.g., dairy processing hall).
- Previous audit reports (to verify CAPA closure).
- Relevant SOPs, SSOPs (Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures), and GMP documents.
- Calibration records of testing instruments (ATP luminometer, thermometers).

**Step 2 — Opening Meeting**

- Conducted with auditee department head.
- Confirms audit scope, methodology, confidentiality, and safety protocols.

**Step 3 — On-Site Audit Execution**

The auditor uses the **"5M + E + L"** technique:
- **Man (Personnel hygiene)** – Uniforms, gloves, hairnets, handwashing, jewelry, health declarations.
- **Machine (Equipment)** – Cleaning validation, sanitation tags, CIP (Clean-in-Place) records.
- **Material (Raw materials)** – Storage hygiene, FIFO, allergen segregation.
- **Method (Process)** – SSOP adherence, sanitization concentration, contact time, temperature.
- **Measurement (Monitoring)** – ATP testing, swab testing, environmental monitoring.
- **Environment** – Lighting, ventilation, drainage, waste disposal, condensation control.
- **Legislation** – FSSAI Schedule 4 compliance, BIS standards, ISO compliance.

**Step 4 — Evidence Collection**

Evidence is collected via:
- **Observation** – Visual inspection of premises.
- **Interview** – Talking with operators and cleaners.
- **Documentation Review** – Cleaning logs, chemical MSDS, calibration certificates.
- **Sampling** – ATP swabs, microbial swabs, water testing.

**Step 5 — NC Classification Logic**

$$
\text{NC Severity} = \begin{cases} \textbf{Critical} & \text{if } P_{risk} \ge 9 \text{ (High prob × High severity)} \\ \textbf{Major} & \text{if } 4 \le P_{risk} < 9 \\ \textbf{Minor} & \text{if } 1 \le P_{risk} < 4 \\ \textbf{OFI} & \text{if } P_{risk} = 0 \text{ but improvement possible} \end{cases}
$$

Where $P_{risk}$ is computed using a **Risk Matrix** (Likelihood × Severity, each scored 1–3).

**Step 6 — Closing Meeting & Report**

- Auditor presents findings to auditee.
- Disagreements are discussed and resolved.
- Report is issued within 5 working days with unique NC numbers.

**Step 7 — CAPA Verification**

$$
CCT = T_{close} - T_{raise}
$$

where $T_{close}$ is the date of verified closure and $T_{raise}$ is the NC raised date. Effectiveness verification may require a **follow-up audit**.

### 3.2 External Audit (Third-Party) — Stage-Wise Logic

A typical **FSSC 22000 / BRCGS certification audit** consists of:

#### Stage 1 Audit (Documentation Review)

$$
\text{Stage 1 Outcome} = \begin{cases} \text{Pass} & \rightarrow \text{Proceed to Stage 2} \\ \text{Fail} & \rightarrow \text{Re-audit after 3 months} \end{cases}
$$

The auditor verifies:
- FSMS documentation completeness.
- Prerequisite Programs (PRPs) — facility design, sanitation, pest control, training, maintenance.
- HACCP plan validity.
- Internal audit and management review records.

#### Stage 2 Audit (On-Site Certification Audit)

The auditor spends **audit days = $\sqrt{N_{employees}}$ (BRCGS rule)** verifying implementation. For a plant with 100 employees:

$$
\text{Audit Days (BRCGS)} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ audit days (minimum 2)}
$$

For FSSC 22000, audit duration is calculated as:
$$
T_{audit} = 0.5 \times N_{employees}^{0.5} + \text{complexity factors}
$$

#### Certification Decision

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \textbf{Certified} & \text{if 0 Critical, } \le 1 \text{ Major, Minor NCs closed within 28 days} \\ \textbf{Suspended} & \text{if Critical or } > 5 \text{ Major NCs} \\ \textbf{Withdrawn} & \text{if continued non-compliance after suspension} \end{cases}
$$

### 3.3 Differences: Internal vs. External Audit (Analytical Matrix)

| Parameter | Internal Audit | External Audit |
|---|---|---|
| **Conducted by** | Own trained employees | Customer / Certification Body |
| **Standard** | Internal SOPs / ISO 22000 (self) | GFSI standards / Customer specs |
| **Frequency** | Planned intervals (min twice/yr) | Annual surveillance + 3-yr recertification |
| **Confidentiality** | Internal report | May be shared publicly (certificates) |
| **Cost** | Low (in-house) | High (₹2–10 lakh per audit) |
| **Legal weight** | Limited | High (regulatory acceptance) |
| **Corrective action** | Internal CAPA | Mandatory closure before certification |
| **Independence** | Independence of area, not organization | Full independence |
| **Focus** | Continuous improvement | Compliance verification |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Internal Sanitation Audit Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Annual Audit Program] --> A2[Prepare Audit Plan and Schedule]
    A2 --> A3[Select Independent Internal Auditor]
    A3 --> A4[Develop Zone Specific Checklist]
    A4 --> A5[Conduct Opening Meeting]
    A5 --> A6[On Site Audit: 5M plus E plus L]
    A6 --> A7[Collect Evidence: Observation, Interview, Records]
    A7 --> A8{Classify Findings}
    A8 -->|Critical| A9[Immediate Stop and Escalate]
    A8 -->|Major| A10[Issue Major NC within 7 days]
    A8 -->|Minor| A11[Issue Minor NC within 30 days]
    A8 -->|OFI| A12[Suggest Improvement]
    A9 --> A13[Issue Audit Report within 5 days]
    A10 --> A13
    A11 --> A13
    A12 --> A13
    A13 --> A14[Implement CAPA]
    A14 --> A15[Verify Effectiveness]
    A15 --> A16{All NCs Closed?}
    A16 -->|Yes| A17[Feed into Management Review]
    A16 -->|No| A14
    A17 --> A18[End: Update Audit Program]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — External Audit Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Food Plant FSMS] --> B2{Audit Type}
    B2 -->|Second Party| B3[Customer Audit]
    B2 -->|Third Party| B4[Certification Body]
    B3 --> B5[Customer Specific Checklist]
    B5 --> B6[Supplier Approval or Rejection]
    B4 --> B7{Standard}
    B7 --> B8[FSSC 22000 v6]
    B7 --> B9[BRCGS Issue 9]
    B7 --> B10[SQF Edition 9]
    B7 --> B11[IFS Food v8]
    B7 --> B12[ISO 22000 only]
    B8 --> B13[Stage 1 Document Review]
    B9 --> B13
    B10 --> B13
    B11 --> B13
    B12 --> B13
    B13 --> B14[Stage 2 On Site Audit]
    B14 --> B15{NC Analysis}
    B15 -->|0 Critical| B16[Certificate Issued Valid 3 Years]
    B15 -->|Critical Found| B17[Suspension or Withdrawal]
    B16 --> B18[Annual Surveillance Audit]
    B18 --> B19[Recertification at Year 3]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Sanitation Audit Scope

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Sanitation and Hygiene Audit))
    Facility Design
      Layout and Zoning
      Drainage
      Lighting and Ventilation
    Cleaning and Sanitization
      SSOP Compliance
      CIP COP Validation
      Chemical Concentration
    Personal Hygiene
      Hand Washing
      Protective Clothing
      Health Declarations
    Pest Control
      IPM Program
      Bait Station Logs
      Trend Analysis
    Environmental Monitoring
      ATP Swabs
      Microbial Swabs
      Allergen Testing
    Waste Management
      Segregation
      Disposal Logs
      Effluent Treatment
    Training and Competence
      Induction Training
      Refresher Modules
      Records
    Documentation
      SOPs SSOPs
      Records Retention
      Change Control
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define an Internal Audit. List any four key characteristics of an internal sanitation audit in a food plant.**

**Model Answer:**
An internal audit is a **systematic, independent, and documented** evaluation conducted by the organization's own personnel to determine whether sanitation and hygiene activities conform to planned arrangements (SOPs, SSOPs, ISO 22000 requirements).

**Four Key Characteristics:**
1. **Independence** – Auditor should not belong to the area being audited.
2. **Documented Process** – Audit plan, checklist, and report are written records.
3. **Planned Frequency** – Conducted at defined intervals (typically twice a year for sanitation).
4. **Corrective Action Oriented** – Findings trigger CAPA and feed into Management Review (Clause 9.2 of ISO 22000).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 0.5 each for 4 characteristics)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] Differentiate between a Second-Party and Third-Party sanitation audit with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Second-Party Audit | Third-Party Audit |
|---|---|---|
| **Conducted by** | Customer or their agent | Independent accredited certification body |
| **Purpose** | Verify contractual hygiene compliance | Certify against a public standard |
| **Example** | Nestlé auditor visiting a spice supplier | FSSC 22000 audit by DNV or SGS |
| **Result** | Supplier approval or rejection | Certificate valid for 3 years |
| **Frequency** | As per contract (often annual) | Annual surveillance + 3-yearly recertification |

*(3 marks: comparison table with 4 points)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Provided — Answer ANY ONE of the Following:**

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for conducting an internal sanitation audit** in a food processing plant. Include the PDCA approach, NC classification logic, and CAPA verification process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the structure and contents of a **Sanitation Audit Checklist** with reference to a dairy processing plant. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Step-by-Step Internal Sanitation Audit Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Establish Audit Program (1 mark):** Define scope (e.g., dairy plant hygiene), frequency (twice/year per ISO 22000), methods, and responsibilities per Clause 9.2.

2. **Select Auditor (0.5 mark):** Must be independent of the audited area and trained as per ISO 19011.

3. **Prepare Checklist (0.5 mark):** Tailored to the area — high-risk zones (raw milk reception, pasteurization) vs. low-risk (packaging).

4. **Opening Meeting (0.5 mark):** Confirm scope, time, criteria, and confidentiality with the auditee.

5. **On-Site Execution (2 marks):** Use the **5M + E + L** technique:
   - Man: hand washing, gloves, health declaration.
   - Machine: CIP records, sanitation tags, gasket condition.
   - Material: cold storage hygiene, FIFO.
   - Method: SSOP compliance, sanitizer concentration (e.g., 200 ppm chlorine).
   - Measurement: ATP test (≤30 RLU), microbial swabs (≤100 CFU/cm² TVC).
   - Environment: drainage, condensation, waste segregation.
   - Legislation: FSSAI Schedule 4.

6. **NC Classification (1 mark):**
   - Critical: Direct food safety risk → immediate production halt.
   - Major: Significant lapse → 7 days.
   - Minor: Isolated issue → 30 days.
   - OFI: Suggestion only.

7. **Audit Report & CAPA Verification (1 mark):** Issue report within 5 working days; verify CAPA closure through re-audit or document review.

8. **Management Review Input (0.5 mark):** Results feed into Clause 9.3 review for continual improvement.

**(b) Sanitation Audit Checklist for Dairy Plant (7 marks):**

| Section | Checkpoint | Standard / Acceptance Criteria |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Personnel Hygiene** | Hand washing frequency | Every entry/exit; ≥20 seconds |
| | Gloves usage | Changed every 2 hours or on contamination |
| | Health declaration | Signed daily before shift |
| | Uniform cleanliness | Daily change; no jewelry |
| **2. Facility & Environment** | Floor drainage | No standing water; slope 1:100 to drain |
| | Wall-ceiling condition | No cracks, mold, flaking paint |
| | Lighting intensity | ≥540 lux in inspection areas |
| | Ventilation filters | Changed quarterly; records maintained |
| **3. Equipment Cleaning** | CIP temperature | 77°C for 10 min minimum |
| | CIP chemical concentration | 1–2% NaOH, then 0.5–1% HNO₃ |
| | Sanitizer spray (post-CIP) | 200 ppm chlorine; contact 1–2 min |
| | Equipment sanitation tag | Date, time, chemical, signature |
| **4. Pest Control** | Bait stations | Every 6–8 m; intact; logged monthly |
| | Fly catchers | UV type; bulbs replaced 6-monthly |
| | Insectocutor placement | 3 m from production line |
| **5. Environmental Monitoring** | ATP swab (post-sanitation) | ≤30 RLU |
| | Total Viable Count | ≤100 CFU/cm² |
| | Coliforms | ≤10 CFU/cm² |
| | Listeria / Salmonella | Absent in 25 g / 100 cm² |
| **6. Waste Management** | Solid waste removal | Every 4 hours during production |
| | Effluent pH | 6.5–7.5 before discharge |
| | Color-coded bins | Black (general), Green (biodegradable), Red (hazardous) |
| **7. Documentation** | Cleaning logs | Updated within 30 min of task |
| | Chemical MSDS | Available at point of use |
| | Calibration of ATP meter | Monthly; certificate available |

*(Each section ~1 mark; include zone-wise breakdown for full marks)*

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **stages of a Third-Party Certification Audit** (e.g., FSSC 22000 or BRCGS). What are the criteria for granting, suspending, or withdrawing certification? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Internal and External Audits** with respect to purpose, scope, frequency, cost, independence, and legal validity. Construct a comparison matrix. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Stages of Third-Party Certification Audit (7 marks):**

1. **Pre-Audit Stage (Optional)** (0.5 mark) – Gap analysis by consultant to prepare the plant.

2. **Application & Contract (0.5 mark)** – Plant submits FSMS documents to Certification Body (CB); contract signed defining audit duration.

3. **Stage 1 – Documentation Review (2 marks):**
   - Auditor reviews FSMS manual, HACCP plan, PRPs (ISO/TS 22002-1), SOPs, SSOPs, internal audit reports, and management review minutes.
   - Verifies readiness for Stage 2.
   - Output: Stage 1 report with findings (none critical expected).

4. **Stage 2 – On-Site Certification Audit (3 marks):**
   - Auditor visits the plant for **$\sqrt{N_{employees}}$** audit days.
   - Conducts opening meeting, walks through all zones (raw material to dispatch).
   - Interviews personnel, reviews records, takes samples (ATP, swabs).
   - Issues findings: Critical, Major, Minor, OFI.
   - Closing meeting held; report issued.

5. **Certification Decision (1 mark):**
   - **Granted:** 0 Critical, ≤1 Major (with closure plan), Minors closed within 28 days.
   - **Suspended:** Critical NC found or >5 Majors.
   - **Withdrawn:** Continued non-compliance 6 months after suspension.

6. **Surveillance Audits (Annual)** (0.5 mark) + **Recertification every 3 years** (0.5 mark).

**(b) Comparison Matrix (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Internal Audit | External Audit |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Internal compliance & improvement | Independent verification & certification |
| **Scope** | Any process/area chosen by management | Defined by standard (entire FSMS) |
| **Frequency** | Min. twice/year (ISO 22000 Clause 9.2) | Annual surveillance + 3-yearly recertification |
| **Auditor** | Own trained employee (independent of area) | External accredited auditor |
| **Cost** | Minimal (in-house) | ₹2–10 lakh per audit event |
| **Independence** | Organizational independence only | Full third-party independence |
| **Legal Validity** | Limited; internal use | High; accepted by regulators & retailers |
| **Output** | Internal audit report | Public certificate & audit report |
| **CAPA timeframe** | Flexible (7–30 days) | Strict (28 days for Minors) |
| **Standards Used** | Internal SOPs + Reference to ISO 22000 | GFSI standards (FSSC, BRCGS, SQF) |

*(Each row 0.5–1 mark; 7 rows ≈ 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often confuse *Second-Party* with *Third-Party* audits. Remember: Second-Party = Customer; Third-Party = Independent Certification Body.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to mention **ISO 19011:2018** as the standard for auditing management systems. This is a 1-mark giveaway in KTU answers.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing "audit" without specifying **"documented"** and **"independent"** — both are mandatory per ISO 19011 definition.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing NC severity thresholds (Critical/Major/Minor) — these are standard but **numbers and timeline may vary slightly** between FSSC, BRCGS, and SQF. Always specify the standard you are referring to.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In 14-mark answers, failing to include **numerical examples** (ATP ≤30 RLU, TVC ≤100 CFU/cm², CIP 77°C) — examiners reward quantification heavily.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

✅ **Definition:** Audit = Systematic + Independent + Documented evaluation against criteria.

✅ **Three Pillars (ISO 19011):** Systematic, Independent, Documented.

✅ **Internal Audit** = First-Party; conducted by own staff; Clause 9.2 of ISO 22000.

✅ **External Audit** = Second-Party (customer) or Third-Party (CB).

✅ **Major Standards:** FSSC 22000 v6, BRCGS Issue 9, SQF Ed. 9, IFS Food v8, ISO 22000:2018.

✅ **Internal Audit Cycle:** PDCA → Plan → Do → Check → Act → Management Review.

✅ **NC Classification:** Critical (immediate halt) | Major (≤7 days) | Minor (≤30 days) | OFI.

✅ **5M + E + L Technique:** Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Environment, Legislation.

✅ **CAPA Closure Time (CCT):** Critical ≤24 h; Major ≤7 days; Minor ≤30 days.

✅ **Sanitation Metrics:** ATP ≤30 RLU; TVC ≤100 CFU/cm²; Pathogen absent in 25 g.

✅ **Audit Duration (BRCGS):** Audit Days = √(Number of Employees); minimum 2 days.

✅ **Certification Validity:** 3 years; annual surveillance; Stage 1 + Stage 2 mandatory.

✅ **Unannounced Audits:** FSSC 22000 v6 mandates at least one unannounced audit in the 3-year cycle.

✅ **FSSAI Reference:** Schedule 4 of FSSAI (2011) defines General Hygienic Practices for food businesses.

✅ **Reporting:** Internal audit report within 5 working days; CAPA closure for certification ≤28 days.

✅ **Difference to Remember:** Internal audit focuses on **continual improvement**; External audit focuses on **compliance verification**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation for Regulatory Inspections and Audits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT747 - FOOD PLANT SANITATION & HYGEINE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Preparation for Regulatory Inspections and Audits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Regulatory Inspection** is a systematic, statutory, and legally authorized examination of a food manufacturing facility, its processes, personnel, and documentation by a designated government regulatory authority (such as **FSSAI**, **USFDA**, **EFSA**, **CFIA**, or **Codex Alimentarius Commission** affiliated bodies) to verify compliance with prevailing food safety laws, regulations, and standards.

**Audit**, in contrast, is a structured, evidence-based evaluation conducted either internally (first-party), by customers (second-party), or by accredited third-party certification bodies (e.g., **ISO 22000**, **BRCGS**, **SQF**, **FSSC 22000**) to assess the conformance and effectiveness of a food safety management system (FSMS) against a defined set of criteria.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the **FSSAI (Food Safety and Standards Authority of India) Act, 2006**, every food business operator (FBO) is legally mandated to allow regulatory inspections, produce records, and comply with **Schedule 4** requirements concerning sanitary and hygienic practices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an inspection as a **"school surprise test"** and an audit as a **"scheduled annual examination"**:

- **Inspection** = A surprise test by the principal. You do not know exactly when, but your books, notes, and conduct must ALWAYS be ready. The inspector walks in unannounced and checks your desk, your answers, your hygiene (literally!).
- **Audit** = A scheduled semester exam where the syllabus, marking scheme, and question paper format are pre-defined. You prepare a "mock exam" (mock audit) beforehand and submit a "report card" (CAPA report).

In a food plant, the "books" are your **SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures)**, **records (CCP monitoring logs, cleaning logs, calibration records)**, and the "uniform" is your **GMP (Good Manufacturing Practices)** and **GHP (Good Hygiene Practices)** compliance on the shop floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Reference:** The three pillars of any audit/inspection are:
> 1. **Documentation Review (Paper audit)** – ~40% weightage
> 2. **Physical Site Inspection (Floor walk-through)** – ~40% weightage  
> 3. **Personnel Interviews (Knowledge verification)** – ~20% weightage

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Standard Metric | Definition |
|---|---|
| **NC (Non-Conformance)** | A deviation from a specified requirement |
| **OFI (Opportunity for Improvement)** | A finding that is not a non-conformance but could improve the system |
| **CAPA** | Corrective and Preventive Action |
| **Critical NC** | A non-conformance affecting food safety requiring immediate action |
| **Major NC** | A significant breach that could lead to product recall |
| **Minor NC** | An isolated or administrative lapse |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phases of Regulatory Inspection and Audit (Structured Logic)

A complete regulatory inspection and audit cycle consists of **six sequential phases**:

### Phase 1: Pre-Inspection / Pre-Audit Notification
- Receipt of **written notification** (audit) or **surprise visit** (regulatory inspection).
- For audits, the **audit agenda, scope, criteria, and standards** are communicated.
- The **Food Safety Team Leader (FSTL)** convenes an emergency meeting.

### Phase 2: Document Review (Opening Meeting)
- The auditor reviews mandatory documents:
  - **FSSAI License / Registration Certificate**
  - **FSMS Manual** (ISO 22000 / FSSC 22000)
  - **HACCP Plan** with CCP critical limits
  - **Prerequisite Programs (PRPs)** and **Operational PRPs (OPRPs)**
  - **Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures (SSOPs)**
  - **Pest Control Records**
  - **Calibration Records** for thermometers, pH meters, weighing balances
  - **Allergen Management Program**
  - **Recall Plan** and **Mock Recall Records**

### Phase 3: Opening Meeting
- Introduction of audit team and plant personnel.
- Confirmation of **audit scope, duration, methodology, and confidentiality clauses**.
- Discussion of **safety protocols** within the plant.

### Phase 4: Floor Walk-Through / Site Inspection
- Physical verification of:
  - **GMP compliance** (building condition, ventilation, lighting)
  - **Hygiene practices** of workers (glove usage, hairnets, footwear)
  - **Pest control measures** (bait stations, insectocutors)
  - **Storage practices** (FIFO/FEFO)
  - **Equipment sanitation** status
  - **Process flow** from raw material to finished goods
  - **CCP monitoring** records and real-time verification

### Phase 5: Personnel Interviews
- Auditors interview workers, supervisors, QA/QC personnel.
- Questions include:
  - "What do you do if the metal detector rejects a batch?"
  - "What is the critical limit for cooking temperature?"
  - "How do you identify an allergen-containing product?"

### Phase 6: Closing Meeting & Reporting
- Auditor presents findings: **Critical NCs, Major NCs, Minor NCs, OFIs**.
- Issuance of **Audit Report** within an agreed timeframe (usually **15 working days**).
- Submission of **CAPA plan** within stipulated time (typically **30 days** for major NCs).

## 2.2 Audit Classification Matrix (KTU High-Yield Reference)

| Audit Type | Conducted By | Purpose | Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **First-Party (Internal)** | Own QA team | Self-assessment | Quarterly |
| **Second-Party** | Customer / Buyer | Supplier approval | Annual or per contract |
| **Third-Party (Certification)** | Accredited CB (e.g., BSI, DNV) | Certification | Annual |
| **Regulatory** | Government body (FSSAI, USFDA) | Legal compliance | Unannounced / Risk-based |

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Phase

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Why:** Audits are not punitive; they are **risk-based verification tools**. The objective is to ensure that the FSMS is **not just documented but effectively implemented** (this is the ISO 22000 clause 9.2 requirement for internal audit).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Process Metrics for Audit Performance)

> [!NOTE]
> Although food safety audits are qualitative, certain **performance metrics** are quantified for continuous improvement.

$$
\text{NC Closure Rate} (\%) = \frac{\text{Number of NCs closed within target date}}{\text{Total NCs raised}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Audit Readiness Score (ARS)} = \frac{(D \times 0.4) + (F \times 0.4) + (P \times 0.2)}{100}
$$

Where:
- $D$ = Documentation compliance score (out of 100)
- $F$ = Floor compliance score (out of 100)
- $P$ = Personnel interview score (out of 100)

$$
\text{CAPA Effectiveness Index} = \frac{\text{Number of NCs not recurring in next audit}}{\text{Total NCs from previous audit}}
$$

**Real-World Utility:** These metrics are used by **Quality Managers** to benchmark plant performance, predict audit outcomes, and demonstrate **continual improvement** to certification bodies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework: Audit Readiness Evaluation

## 3.1 Mathematical Modeling of Audit Preparedness

Let us derive the **Audit Preparedness Index (API)** used by senior QA managers in food plants.

### Step 1: Define the Constituent Parameters

Consider five critical pillars of audit readiness:

$$
\text{API} = w_1 \cdot S_{\text{doc}} + w_2 \cdot S_{\text{floor}} + w_3 \cdot S_{\text{personnel}} + w_4 \cdot S_{\text{records}} + w_5 \cdot S_{\text{CAPA}}
$$

Where $w_i$ are the weightages assigned to each pillar, and $\sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i = 1$.

### Step 2: Assign Standard KTU Weightages

Based on industry practice (GFSI benchmarked):

$$
w_1 = 0.30, \quad w_2 = 0.25, \quad w_3 = 0.15, \quad w_4 = 0.20, \quad w_5 = 0.10
$$

### Step 3: Numerical Evaluation Example

A sample food plant scores the following in a mock audit:

$$
S_{\text{doc}} = 85, \quad S_{\text{floor}} = 78, \quad S_{\text{personnel}} = 92, \quad S_{\text{records}} = 88, \quad S_{\text{CAPA}} = 75
$$

### Step 4: Compute the API

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{API} &= (0.30 \times 85) + (0.25 \times 78) + (0.15 \times 92) + (0.20 \times 88) + (0.10 \times 75) \\
&= 25.5 + 19.5 + 13.8 + 17.6 + 7.5 \\
&= 83.9
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Interpret the Result

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{API} \geq 90 &\Rightarrow \text{Audit-Ready (Excellent)} \\
80 \leq \text{API} < 90 &\Rightarrow \text{Conditionally Ready (Good)} \\
70 \leq \text{API} < 80 &\Rightarrow \text{Needs Improvement} \\
\text{API} < 70 &\Rightarrow \text{Not Ready (Reschedule Audit)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** With API = 83.9, the plant is **Conditionally Ready**; documentation and CAPA need strengthening before the regulatory visit.

### Step 6: CAPA Closure Time Calculation

FSSAI mandates closure of **Critical NCs within 7 days** and **Major NCs within 30 days**.

$$
\text{CAPA Cycle Time (CCT)} = T_{\text{NC raised}} - T_{\text{NC verified closed}}
$$

$$
\text{Average CCT} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(T_{\text{closed},i} - T_{\text{raised},i})
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** A low CCT indicates a responsive quality system. Industry benchmark: **Average CCT ≤ 14 days** for a world-class food plant.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Audit Lifecycle Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Audit Notification] --> B[Step B: Document Review]
    B --> C[Step C: Opening Meeting]
    C --> D[Step D: Floor Walk Through]
    D --> E[Step E: Personnel Interview]
    E --> F[Step F: Closing Meeting]
    F --> G{Step G: NCs Identified}
    G -->|Yes| H[Step H: Issue Audit Report]
    G -->|No| I[Step I: Issue Compliance Certificate]
    H --> J[Step J: Submit CAPA Plan]
    J --> K[Step K: CAPA Verification]
    K --> L[Step L: Audit Closure]
    I --> L
    L --> M[Step M: Continual Improvement]
```

## 4.2 Pre-Audit Preparation Checklist (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Block1["DOCUMENT PREPARATION"]
        D1[FSSAI License]
        D2[HACCP Plan]
        D3[FSMS Manual]
        D4[SOPs and SSOPs]
        D5[Calibration Records]
    end
    
    subgraph Block2["FLOOR PREPARATION"]
        F1[GMP Compliance]
        F2[Pest Control Status]
        F3[Equipment Sanitation]
        F4[Personal Hygiene Check]
        F5[Storage Practices]
    end
    
    subgraph Block3["PERSONNEL PREPARATION"]
        P1[Training Records]
        P2[Mock Interviews]
        P3[Hygiene Dress Code]
        P4[Role Assignments]
    end
    
    subgraph Block4["MOCK AUDIT"]
        M1[Internal Auditor Selection]
        M2[Scope Definition]
        M3[Findings Documentation]
        M4[Pre CAPA Closure]
    end
    
    Block1 --> M1
    Block2 --> M1
    Block3 --> M1
    Block1 --> M2
    Block2 --> M2
    Block3 --> M2
    M1 --> M3
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
```

## 4.3 CAPA Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    NC1[NC Identified] --> NC2[Root Cause Analysis 5 Why]
    NC2 --> NC3[Immediate Corrective Action]
    NC3 --> NC4[Preventive Action Plan]
    NC4 --> NC5[Implementation]
    NC5 --> NC6[Verification of Effectiveness]
    NC6 --> NC7{Action Effective}
    NC7 -->|Yes| NC8[NC Closed]
    NC7 -->|No| NC2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define a regulatory inspection. How does it differ from a third-party certification audit?**

**Model Answer:**

A **regulatory inspection** is a legal, statutory examination of a food facility conducted by a government authority (e.g., FSSAI, USFDA) under specific food safety laws (such as the FSSAI Act 2006) to verify mandatory legal compliance.

A **third-party certification audit** (e.g., ISO 22000, BRCGS) is conducted by an accredited independent certification body to assess conformance against a voluntary or contractual standard.

| Parameter | Regulatory Inspection | Third-Party Audit |
|---|---|---|
| Legal basis | Statutory (mandatory) | Voluntary / contractual |
| Authority | Government body | Accredited CB |
| Frequency | Risk-based, often unannounced | Pre-scheduled, annual |
| Outcome | Prosecution, license cancellation | Certificate issuance |
| Cost | Borne by government | Borne by plant |

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2023]
**List any six documents that must be kept ready before a regulatory inspection.**

**Model Answer:**

Six essential documents for regulatory inspection readiness:
1. Valid **FSSAI License / Registration** (Form A or Form B)
2. **HACCP Plan** with CCP critical limits and monitoring records
3. **FSMS Manual** (as per ISO 22000:2018)
4. **Sanitation Standard Operating Procedures (SSOPs)**
5. **Calibration Records** for thermometers, pH meters, weighing scales
6. **Pest Control Records** and **Allergen Management Program** records

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **phases of a regulatory inspection** in a food processing plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **components of an effective CAPA system** with reference to root cause analysis tools. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

The regulatory inspection consists of **six systematic phases**:

**Phase 1: Notification or Arrival**
- For announced inspections, FSSAI issues prior intimation.
- For surprise inspections, the inspecting officer (Food Safety Officer - FSO) arrives unannounced as per Section 38 of the FSSAI Act, 2006.

**Phase 2: Document Review**
The FSO examines:
- **FSSAI license validity**
- **Product approval records**
- **Lab test reports** (internal and external)
- **HACCP and FSMS documentation**
- **Recall traceability records**

**Phase 3: Opening Meeting**
The FSO explains the scope, methodology, and expected duration. The plant's Food Safety Team Leader briefs the FSO on plant layout and processes.

**Phase 4: Floor Walk-Through**
The FSO physically inspects:
- Raw material receiving area (temperature of cold chain)
- Processing area (GMP, hygiene of workers)
- Packaging area (labelling compliance, FSSAI logo, batch coding)
- Storage area (FIFO compliance, segregation of allergens)

**Phase 5: Sample Collection and Personnel Interview**
- The FSO may draw samples under **Section 47** of FSSAI Act for lab analysis.
- Workers are interviewed on hygiene practices, allergen handling, and CCP monitoring.

**Phase 6: Closing Meeting and Report**
- Findings are discussed, **show-cause notices** may be issued.
- Plant is given **7 to 14 days** to respond with CAPA.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action)** is a structured problem-solving system.

**Components of CAPA:**

1. **NC Identification:** Document the deviation clearly with date, location, and evidence.

2. **Root Cause Analysis (RCA):** Use tools such as:
   - **5-Why Analysis:** Iteratively ask "why" five times to reach root cause.
   - **Fishbone Diagram (Ishikawa):** Categories - Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Environment.
   - **Fault Tree Analysis:** For complex process failures.

3. **Immediate Corrective Action:** Containment of the problem (e.g., quarantine of affected batch).

4. **Preventive Action:** Long-term measure to prevent recurrence (e.g., SOP revision, training).

5. **Implementation:** Execute the action plan with timeline and responsibility.

6. **Verification of Effectiveness:** Confirm through follow-up audit that the issue is resolved.

**Example Application:**
If a Critical NC is raised for CCP temperature deviation in pasteurization:
- **5-Why Root Cause:** Why did temperature drop? → Thermostat failed. Why? → Not calibrated. Why? → Calibration SOP not followed. Why? → No trained operator. Why? → Training schedule lapsed.
- **Corrective Action:** Recalibrate thermostat, retrain operator.
- **Preventive Action:** Implement monthly calibration log with supervisor verification.

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2022, Modified]

**(a)** Describe the **preparation strategy for a third-party food safety audit** (ISO 22000 / BRCGS). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **classification of non-conformances** and their impact on certification status. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Pre-Audit Preparation Strategy (8 Steps):**

1. **Scope Confirmation:** Verify the audit scope matches the certification requested (e.g., Food Manufacturing Category).

2. **Document Review:** Ensure FSMS manual, procedures, and records are updated and version-controlled.

3. **Conduct Mock Audit:** Internal auditors perform a full-scope mock audit **60 days before** the certification audit.

4. **Close Prior NCs:** Verify all NCs from previous surveillance audits are closed with evidence.

5. **Calibration Verification:** Check calibration certificates are valid for all measuring instruments.

6. **Pest Control Audit:** Engage pest control agency to inspect and certify the facility.

7. **Training Records:** Ensure all employees have undergone food safety training (HACCP level 1-4).

8. **Recall Plan Testing:** Conduct a **mock recall** within the last 12 months and document traceability time (industry benchmark: < 2 hours).

**Auditor Interaction Tips:**
- Be honest and transparent.
- Do not hide documents.
- Ensure escorts are trained in audit etiquette.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Classification of Non-Conformances:**

| Type | Definition | Example | Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Critical NC** | Immediate food safety risk; system failure | Presence of pesticide residue beyond MRL, pest infestation in production | Certificate withheld or suspended; product recall |
| **Major NC** | Significant breach of prerequisite programs | Absence of allergen control program; incomplete HACCP records | Certification not granted until closure |
| **Minor NC** | Isolated, administrative lapse | Missing signature on a single record | Must be closed in next surveillance |

**Impact on Certification Status:**
- **Zero Critical NCs** → Certification possible after CAPA closure of Major NCs.
- **One Critical NC** → Certification suspended; re-audit required.
